docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Check return values - Most C FFI functions return error codes. I recommend verifying them.
  2. Close resources - Call cleanup functions such as sqlite3_close or SDL_DestroyWindow.
  3. Use opaque types - Use these for C pointers like SDL_Window*.
  4. Import with aliases - Use as IO or as DB to prevent namespace collisions.
  5. Check module.json - Verify build dependencies and system requirements.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• My main documentation: docs/FEATURES.md
  • My example programs: examples/
  • Module-specific docs: modules/<name>/README.md
  • My build system: docs/MODULE_FORMAT.md

docs.title: Creating Modules
docs.text: I show you how to create custom modules to integrate C libraries with me.

docs.title: My Module Structure
docs.text:
Every module I use lives in modules/<name>/ and contains:

modules/mymodule/
├── module.json          # Module metadata and build configuration
├── mymodule.nano        # My FFI declarations
├── mymodule_helpers.c   # Optional: C helper functions
└── mymodule_helpers.nano # Optional: My helper function declarations

docs.title: Step 1: Create Module Directory
docs.text: mkdir modules/mymodule

docs.title: Step 2: Create module.json
docs.text: The module.json file tells my build system how to compile and link your module.

docs.title: Example: Pure FFI Module (no C code)
docs.text:
{
  "name": "stdio",
  "c_sources": [],
  "dependencies": []
}

docs.title: Example: Module with C Helpers
docs.text:
{
  "name": "sdl_helpers",
  "c_sources": ["sdl_helpers.c"],
  "dependencies": ["sdl"]
}

docs.title: Example: Module with External Library
docs.text:
{
  "name": "sdl_mixer",
  "pkg_config": ["SDL2_mixer"],
  "dependencies": ["sdl"]
}

docs.title: Full Configuration Options
docs.text:
{
  "name": "mymodule",           // Module name (matches directory)
  "c_sources": [                // C files to compile (optional)
    "mymodule_helpers.c"
  ],
  "pkg_config": [                // System libraries via pkg-config (optional)
    "libname"
  ],
  "link_flags": [                // Manual linker flags (optional)
    "-framework CoreAudio"
  ],
  "dependencies": [              // Other modules I require (optional)
    "sdl"
  ]
}

docs.title: Step 3: Create FFI Declarations (mymodule.nano)
docs.text:
Declare external C functions with extern fn:

# Simple functions
extern fn c_function_name(arg1: int, arg2: string) -> int

# Void return
extern fn do_something(value: float) -> void

# Pointer types (use int)
extern fn get_pointer() -> int
extern fn use_pointer(ptr: int) -> void

# Constants
let MY_CONSTANT: int = 42
let MY_FLAG_A: int = 1
let MY_FLAG_B: int = 2

docs.title: Type Mapping
docs.text: My Type C Equivalent int int64_t float double string const char* bool bool (0 or 1) void void (pointers) Cast to/from int

docs.title: Step 4: Optional C Helpers
docs.text:
If you need custom C code, create mymodule_helpers.c:

#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

// Helper function I can call
int64_t nl_mymodule_helper(int64_t arg1, const char* arg2) {
    // Your C code here
    return 42;
}

Then declare it in mymodule_helpers.nano:

extern fn nl_mymodule_helper(arg1: int, arg2: string) -> int

docs.title: Naming Convention
docs.text:
• Prefix C functions with nl_ to avoid name collisions.
  • Use descriptive names: nl_sdl_render_fill_rect not nl_rfr.

docs.title: Step 5: Using Your Module
docs.text:
In the code you write for me:

import "modules/mymodule/mymodule.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    let result: int = (c_function_name 10 "hello")
    return 0
}

docs.text:
{
  "name": "stdio",
  "c_sources": [],
  "dependencies": []
}

docs.title: modules/stdio/stdio.nano
docs.text:
# File operations
extern fn fopen(filename: string, mode: string) -> int
extern fn fclose(file: int) -> int
extern fn fread(ptr: int, size: int, count: int, file: int) -> int
extern fn fseek(file: int, offset: int, whence: int) -> int
extern fn ftell(file: int) -> int

# Constants
let SEEK_SET: int = 0
let SEEK_CUR: int = 1
let SEEK_END: int = 2
let FILE_MODE_READ: string = "rb"

# Helper function in my syntax
fn file_size(filename: string) -> int {
    let file: int = (fopen filename FILE_MODE_READ)
    if (== file 0) {
        return -1
    } else {
        (fseek file 0 SEEK_END)
        let size: int = (ftell file)
        (fclose file)
        return size
    }
}

# I require shadow tests for my functions
shadow file_size {
    assert (>= (file_size "/nonexistent") -1)
}

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import "modules/stdio/stdio.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    let size: int = (file_size "myfile.txt")
    (println size)
    return 0
}

docs.title: modules/sdl_helpers/sdl_helpers.c
docs.text:
#include <SDL2/SDL.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

// Fill rectangle helper
void nl_sdl_render_fill_rect(SDL_Renderer* renderer, 
                              int64_t x, int64_t y, 
                              int64_t w, int64_t h) {
    SDL_Rect rect = {(int)x, (int)y, (int)w, (int)h};
    SDL_RenderFillRect(renderer, &rect);
}

// Check if window should close
bool nl_sdl_window_should_close() {
    SDL_Event event;
    while (SDL_PollEvent(&event)) {
        if (event.type == SDL_QUIT) {
            return true;
        }
    }
    return false;
}

docs.title: modules/sdl_helpers/sdl_helpers.nano
docs.text:
extern fn nl_sdl_render_fill_rect(renderer: int, x: int, y: int, w: int, h: int) -> void
extern fn nl_sdl_window_should_close() -> bool

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
import "modules/sdl_helpers/sdl_helpers.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    (SDL_Init 32)
    let window: int = (SDL_CreateWindow "Test" 100 100 800 600 4)
    let renderer: int = (SDL_CreateRenderer window -1 6)

    let mut running: bool = true
    while running {
        (SDL_SetRenderDrawColor renderer 255 0 0 255)
        (SDL_RenderClear renderer)

        (nl_sdl_render_fill_rect renderer 100 100 200 200)

        (SDL_RenderPresent renderer)
        (SDL_Delay 16)

        if (nl_sdl_window_should_close) {
            set running false
        } else {}
    }

    (SDL_DestroyRenderer renderer)
    (SDL_DestroyWindow window)
    (SDL_Quit)
    return 0
}

docs.title: Automatic Building

I build modules automatically when you use them:

./bin/nano examples/myprogram.nano

# Compile to native binary
./bin/nanoc examples/myprogram.nano -o myprogram
./myprogram

docs.title: 1. Start Simple
docs.text:
Begin with pure FFI bindings:

extern fn existing_c_function(arg: int) -> int

docs.title: 2. Add Helpers as Needed
docs.text: Write C helpers only when: - The C API is too complex for direct FFI. - You must marshal data structures. - Performance requires a C implementation.

docs.title: 3. Use Descriptive Names
docs.text:
# Good
extern fn nl_sdl_render_filled_rectangle(...)

# Bad
extern fn rfr(...)

docs.title: 4. Document Your Module
docs.text:
Add comments explaining what each function does.

# Load audio file from disk
# Returns: audio handle (int) or 0 on failure
# Example: let audio: int = (load_audio "sound.wav")
extern fn load_audio(filename: string) -> int

docs.title: 5. Add Shadow Tests
docs.text:
I require you to test your helper functions.

fn clamp(value: int, min_val: int, max_val: int) -> int {
    if (< value min_val) {
        return min_val
    } else {
        if (> value max_val) {
            return max_val
        } else {
            return value
        }
    }
}

shadow clamp {
    assert (== (clamp 5 0 10) 5)
    assert (== (clamp -5 0 10) 0)
    assert (== (clamp 15 0 10) 10)
}

docs.title: Pattern 1: Opaque Handles
docs.text:
C libraries often return pointers. I treat them as int.

# SDL_Window* becomes int
extern fn SDL_CreateWindow(...) -> int

# Use the handle
let window: int = (SDL_CreateWindow "Title" 0 0 800 600 4)
if (== window 0) {
    (println "Failed to create window")
} else {
    # Use window
    (SDL_DestroyWindow window)
}

docs.title: Pattern 2: Flags and Constants
docs.text:
# Define flags as constants
let SDL_INIT_VIDEO: int = 32
let SDL_INIT_AUDIO: int = 16
let SDL_INIT_EVERYTHING: int = 62097

# Combine with bitwise OR (use + for now)
let flags: int = (+ SDL_INIT_VIDEO SDL_INIT_AUDIO)
(SDL_Init flags)

docs.title: Pattern 3: Error Checking
docs.text:
extern fn SDL_GetError() -> string

fn init_sdl() -> bool {
    let result: int = (SDL_Init 32)
    if (!= result 0) {
        (println "SDL_Init failed:")
        (println (SDL_GetError))
        return false
    } else {
        return true
    }
}

docs.title: Pattern 4: Resource Management
docs.text:
fn load_and_process_file(filename: string) -> bool {
    let file: int = (fopen filename "rb")
    if (== file 0) {
        return false
    } else {
        # Process file
        # ...

        # Always cleanup
        (fclose file)
        return true
    }
}

docs.title: Module Not Found
docs.text:
Error: Cannot find module 'mymodule'

Check that: 1. Directory exists: modules/mymodule/. 2. module.json exists. 3. mymodule.nano exists. 4. Import path is correct: import "modules/mymodule/mymodule.nano".

docs.title: Linking Errors
docs.text:
Undefined symbols: _my_function
  1. Add function to c_sources in module.json.
  2. Add library to pkg_config.
  3. Add to link_flags.

docs.title: Type Mismatches
docs.text:
Error: Type mismatch in function call

Check my type mapping: - C int to my int (int64_t). - C double to my float. - C char* to my string. - C pointers to my int.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
I provide working modules here: - modules/sdl/ - SDL2 bindings. - modules/sdl_helpers/ - SDL2 with C helpers. - modules/sdl_mixer/ - External library (SDL2_mixer). - modules/stdio/ - Standard C library bindings. - modules/math_ext/ - Math extensions.

All examples in my examples/ directory demonstrate how I use modules.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Browse my existing modules for examples.
  2. Create your first simple FFI module.
  3. Add C helpers if you need them.
  4. Document your module.
  5. Share it.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
• Module Format / System
  • SDL Extensions Guide
  • Standard Library Reference
  • FFI Best Practices

I wish you success with your modules.

docs.title: Module Dependency Management
docs.text: This document explains how NanoLang handles module dependencies and provides guidance for building with optional system libraries.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
NanoLang has a modular architecture where some modules require external system libraries (SDL2, OpenGL, SQLite, etc.). The build system provides tools to validate these dependencies and offers two build modes:

  1. Strict Mode (make examples): Fails if any module dependencies are missing
  2. Graceful Mode (make examples-available): Skips examples with missing dependencies

docs.title: Validate All Modules
docs.text:
make -B modules

This command: - ✅ Shows which modules have all required dependencies - ❌ Shows which modules have missing dependencies - 📦 Provides installation commands for missing packages - ⚠️ Exits with error code 1 if any dependencies are missing

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
Module Validation
========================================

Checking system dependencies for modules...

  ✓ vector2d
  ✓ math_ext
  ✗ sdl (missing: sdl2,sdl2_mixer)
  ✗ readline (missing: readline)

Summary
========================================
Total modules with dependencies: 16
Available: 2
Missing dependencies: 14

docs.title: Strict Mode (Recommended for Development)
docs.text:
make examples

Behavior: - Validates module dependencies first (make modules) - Fails immediately if any dependencies are missing - Ensures you know exactly what's needed - Best for CI/CD and production builds

Use when: - Setting up a new development environment - Preparing for a release - Running CI/CD pipelines - You want explicit dependency management

docs.title: Graceful Mode (Convenient for Quick Iteration)
docs.text:
make examples-available

Behavior: - Builds all examples that can be built - Skips examples with missing dependencies - Always succeeds (exit code 0) - Useful for partial builds

Use when: - Quick testing without installing all dependencies - Working on core features that don't need graphics/audio - Building on minimal systems - You know which examples you need

docs.title: All Dependencies (Full Build)
docs.text:
For a complete build with all modules on Ubuntu/WSL2:

sudo apt-get install -y \
  pkg-config \
  libsdl2-dev \
  libsdl2-image-dev \
  libsdl2-mixer-dev \
  libsdl2-ttf-dev \
  libgl1-mesa-dev \
  libglew-dev \
  libglfw3-dev \
  freeglut3-dev \
  libncurses-dev \
  libreadline-dev \
  libsqlite3-dev \
  libcurl4-openssl-dev \
  libuv1-dev \
  libbullet-dev

docs.title: Minimal Dependencies (Core Only)
docs.text:
For just the core compiler and standard library:

sudo apt-get install -y build-essential python3
make build
make test

docs.title: Module-Specific Dependencies
docs.text:
The make modules command shows exactly what's needed for each module:

$ make -B modules

  • readline
    Install: sudo apt-get install libreadline-dev
  • sdl
    Install: sudo apt-get install libsdl2-dev libsdl2-mixer-dev

docs.title: No External Dependencies
docs.text: These modules work everywhere: - std - Standard library (files, process, env) - math_ext - Extended math functions - vector2d - 2D vector math - stdlib - Legacy stdlib utilities - And more...

docs.title: Graphics & Windowing
docs.text: Require system graphics libraries: - sdl - SDL2 window/graphics/input - sdl_image, sdl_mixer, sdl_ttf - SDL2 extensions - opengl - OpenGL 3D rendering - glfw - OpenGL window management - glew, glut - OpenGL utilities

docs.title: Terminal UI
docs.text:
• ncurses - Terminal UI with colors/cursor control
  • readline - Line editing with history

docs.title: Databases & Networking
docs.text:
• sqlite - Embedded SQL database
  • curl - HTTP client
  • http_server, uv - HTTP server and async I/O

docs.title: Physics & Specialized
docs.text:
• bullet - Bullet physics engine
  • pybridge - Python interop

docs.title: Setting Up a New Development Environment
docs.text:
# 1. Check what's needed
make -B modules

# 2. Install dependencies
sudo apt-get install -y [packages from above]

# 3. Build everything
make build
make test
make examples

docs.title: Working Without Graphics Libraries
docs.text:
# Build core compiler
make build

# Run tests (most will pass)
make test

# Build only available examples
make examples-available

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text:
# GitHub Actions example
- name: Install dependencies
  run: |
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y build-essential python3 pkg-config
    # Add other dependencies as needed

- name: Validate modules
  run: make -B modules

- name: Build examples (strict)
  run: make examples

docs.title: "make modules" shows dependencies missing
docs.text: Solution: Install the packages shown in the output, then run make -B modules again.

docs.title: "make examples" fails
docs.text:
Option 1: Install missing dependencies 

# Follow the commands shown by 'make modules'
sudo apt-get install [missing-packages]

Option 2: Use graceful mode 

make examples-available

docs.title: Module build fails even with packages installed
docs.text:
1. Verify pkg-config can find the package: 

    pkg-config --exists sdl2 && echo "Found" || echo "Not found"

  2. Check package names: 

    dpkg -l | grep libsdl2

  3. Reinstall pkg-config: 

    sudo apt-get install --reinstall pkg-config

docs.title: WSL2
docs.text:
• Graphics modules require WSLg (Windows 11) or X11 forwarding (Windows 10)
  • See BUILDING_WSL2.md for detailed setup

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
• Use Homebrew for dependencies: brew install sdl2 readline ...
  • Some packages have different names (e.g., freeglut vs glut)

docs.title: FreeBSD
docs.text:
• Use pkg: pkg install sdl2 readline ...
  • Package names may differ slightly from Linux

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BUILDING_WSL2.md - WSL2-specific build guide
  • MODULE_SYSTEM.md - Module system architecture
  • PLATFORM_COMPATIBILITY.md - Cross-platform notes

docs.title: My Module System Migration Guide
docs.text: From: import syntax with scattered unsafe {} blocks To: module syntax with module-level safety Status: Phase 1 Complete Date: 2025-01-08

docs.title: My Summary
docs.text:
I have redesigned my module system to make safety explicit at module boundaries. You no longer need to scatter safety markers throughout your code. This change removes 98% of unsafe {} blocks while I maintain the same safety guarantees.

The change: You mark entire modules as unsafe when you import them, instead of marking individual function calls.

docs.title: Safe Modules (My Own Code)
docs.text:
Before: 

import "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano" as Vec

After: 

module "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano" as Vec

Action: Replace import with module.

docs.title: Unsafe Modules (FFI and External Libraries)
docs.text:
Before: 

import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"

fn render() -> void {
    unsafe { (SDL_Init 0) }
    unsafe { (SDL_CreateWindow "Game" 800 600) }
    unsafe { (SDL_Quit) }
}

After: 

unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"

fn render() -> void {
    (SDL_Init 0)
    (SDL_CreateWindow "Game" 800 600)
    (SDL_Quit)
}

Actions: 1. Add unsafe before the module import. 2. Remove all unsafe {} blocks for calls to that module.

docs.title: Example 1: SDL Game
docs.text:
Before (My Old System): 

import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
import "modules/sdl_helpers/sdl_helpers.nano"
import "modules/ui_widgets/ui_widgets.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    unsafe { (SDL_Init 0) }

    let mut window: SDL_Window = 0
    unsafe {
        set window (SDL_CreateWindow "My Game" 800 600)
    }

    let mut running: bool = true
    while running {
        unsafe {
            set running (not (SDL_QuitRequested))
        }

        unsafe { (SDL_RenderClear window) }
        # ... game logic ...
        unsafe { (SDL_RenderPresent window) }
        unsafe { (SDL_Delay 16) }
    }

    unsafe { (SDL_DestroyWindow window) }
    unsafe { (SDL_Quit) }

    return 0
}

After (My New System): 

unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
unsafe module "modules/sdl_helpers/sdl_helpers.nano"
module "modules/ui_widgets/ui_widgets.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    (SDL_Init 0)

    let mut window: SDL_Window = (SDL_CreateWindow "My Game" 800 600)

    let mut running: bool = true
    while running {
        set running (not (SDL_QuitRequested))

        (SDL_RenderClear window)
        # ... game logic ...
        (SDL_RenderPresent window)
        (SDL_Delay 16)
    }

    (SDL_DestroyWindow window)
    (SDL_Quit)

    return 0
}

My Results: - Unsafe blocks: 7 down to 0 (100% reduction) - Lines of code: 35 down to 27 (23% reduction) - Safety: My compile-time guarantees remain unchanged - Clarity: I make safety explicit at the module boundary

docs.title: Example 2: Bullet Physics
docs.text:
Before: 

import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
import "modules/bullet/bullet.nano"

fn init_physics() -> btDynamicsWorld {
    let mut world: btDynamicsWorld = 0
    unsafe {
        set world (btCreateDynamicsWorld)
    }

    let mut gravity: btVector3 = btVector3 { x: 0.0, y: -9.81, z: 0.0 }
    unsafe {
        (btSetGravity world gravity)
    }

    return world
}

fn step_physics(world: btDynamicsWorld, dt: float) -> void {
    unsafe {
        (btStepSimulation world dt)
    }
}

After: 

unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
unsafe module "modules/bullet/bullet.nano"

fn init_physics() -> btDynamicsWorld {
    let mut world: btDynamicsWorld = (btCreateDynamicsWorld)
    let mut gravity: btVector3 = btVector3 { x: 0.0, y: -9.81, z: 0.0 }
    (btSetGravity world gravity)
    return world
}

fn step_physics(world: btDynamicsWorld, dt: float) -> void {
    (btStepSimulation world dt)
}

My Results: - Unsafe blocks: 3 down to 0 (100% reduction) - Cleaner function bodies: I removed the safety noise - Same semantics: I still mark FFI as unsafe, but at the module level

docs.title: Pattern 1: Safe Modules Only
docs.text:
# Before
import "modules/math/math.nano"
import "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano"

# After
module "modules/math/math.nano"
module "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano"

Action: Use find-and-replace to change import to module.

docs.title: Pattern 2: Mixed Safe and Unsafe Modules
docs.text:
# Before
import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"          # Unsafe (FFI)
import "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano"  # Safe (NanoLang)

# After
unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"      # Unsafe (FFI)
module "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano"   # Safe (NanoLang)

Action: I require an unsafe prefix for FFI modules. Use module for the others.

docs.title: Pattern 3: Nested Unsafe Blocks
docs.text:
# Before
import "modules/curl/curl.nano"

fn fetch_url(url: string) -> string {
    let mut handle: CURL = 0
    unsafe {
        set handle (curl_easy_init)
        if (!= handle 0) {
            unsafe { (curl_easy_setopt handle CURLOPT_URL url) }
            unsafe { (curl_easy_perform handle) }
            unsafe { (curl_easy_cleanup handle) }
        }
    }
    return ""
}

# After
unsafe module "modules/curl/curl.nano"

fn fetch_url(url: string) -> string {
    let mut handle: CURL = (curl_easy_init)
    if (!= handle 0) {
        (curl_easy_setopt handle CURLOPT_URL url)
        (curl_easy_perform handle)
        (curl_easy_cleanup handle)
    }
    return ""
}

Action: Remove all unsafe {} blocks after you add unsafe module.

docs.title: Script 1: Update Import Statements
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# Replace 'import' with 'module' for safe modules
# Add 'unsafe' prefix for FFI modules

for file in examples/*.nano; do
    # Safe modules
    sed -i '' 's/^import "modules\/math\//module "modules\/math\//g' "$file"
    sed -i '' 's/^import "modules\/vector2d\//module "modules\/vector2d\//g' "$file"

    # Unsafe modules (FFI)
    sed -i '' 's/^import "modules\/sdl\//unsafe module "modules\/sdl\//g' "$file"
    sed -i '' 's/^import "modules\/bullet\//unsafe module "modules\/bullet\//g' "$file"
    sed -i '' 's/^import "modules\/curl\//unsafe module "modules\/curl\//g' "$file"
done

docs.title: Script 2: Remove Unnecessary Unsafe Blocks
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# See: /tmp/remove_unsafe_blocks.py
# This script removes unsafe {} wrappers.
# I designed it to preserve content and indentation.

docs.title: Definitely Unsafe (FFI and External C Libraries)
docs.text:
• sdl (SDL2 graphics library)
  • bullet (Bullet physics engine)
  • curl (HTTP client library)
  • opengl (OpenGL graphics)
  • glfw (Window and input library)
  • ncurses (Terminal UI library)
  • sqlite (Database library)

docs.title: Definitely Safe (My Own Code)
docs.text:
• vector2d (2D vector math)
  • math (Extended math functions)
  • ui_widgets (UI components)
  • stdlib (Standard library utilities)

docs.title: How to Tell
docs.text:
I suggest checking if the module has extern fn declarations or depends on C libraries:

# If you see this, it's unsafe:
extern fn SDL_Init(flags: int) -> int
extern fn curl_easy_init() -> CURL

# If you see only NanoLang code, it's safe:
fn vec_add(a: Vec2, b: Vec2) -> Vec2 { ... }

docs.title: Q: What if I mix safe and unsafe modules?
docs.text:
A: I handle this by letting you mark each module individually. 

unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"      # Unsafe
module "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano"   # Safe

docs.title: Q: Can I still use unsafe {} blocks?
docs.text:
A: I allow it, but I discourage it. I prefer module-level safety. 

# Discouraged (the old way)
module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
fn render() -> void {
    unsafe { (SDL_Init 0) }
}

# Preferred (the new way)
unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
fn render() -> void {
    (SDL_Init 0)
}

docs.title: Q: What about legacy import syntax?
docs.text:
A: I still support it for now, but I will deprecate it. Please migrate to module. 

# Legacy (I still support this)
import "modules/old.nano"

# Modern (I prefer this)
module "modules/old.nano"

docs.title: Q: What if my module imports other modules?
docs.text:
A: I require each file to declare its own imports. I do not provide transitive safety. 

# file1.nano
unsafe module "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"
# You can use SDL functions here without unsafe blocks

# file2.nano
module "file1.nano"
# You cannot use SDL functions here without unsafe blocks
# You must import SDL directly or mark file1 as unsafe

docs.title: My Migration Checklist
docs.text:
For each project you have built with me:

  • [ ] Step 1: Identify which modules are FFI or unsafe.
  • [ ] Step 2: Add the unsafe prefix to those module imports.
  • [ ] Step 3: Change import to module for all imports.
  • [ ] Step 4: Remove unsafe {} blocks from functions that use unsafe modules.
  • [ ] Step 5: Compile and test your code.
  • [ ] Step 6: Verify that you have no safety regressions.

docs.title: Statistics (My Examples Migration)
docs.text:
Files Updated: 103 example files Imports Changed: 181 import statements Unsafe Modules: 147 (SDL, Bullet, OpenGL, and others) Safe Modules: 34 (vector2d, math, ui_widgets) Unsafe Blocks Removed: 1,235 down to 88 (98% reduction)

Build Status: All my examples compile and run.

docs.title: My Benefits Summary
docs.text: Metric Before After Improvement Unsafe blocks in SDL examples 1,235 88 98% reduction Lines of boilerplate ~3,500 ~250 93% reduction Safety declarations Scattered At module boundary Centralized Code readability Cluttered Clean Improved Compile-time safety Yes Yes Preserved

docs.title: Phase 2: Module Introspection
docs.text:
• I will allow you to query module metadata at compile-time.
  • I will add ___module_info_<name>() functions.
  • This enables me to support advanced tooling.

docs.title: Phase 3: Warning System
docs.text:
• --warn-unsafe-imports: I will warn on any unsafe module.
  • --warn-ffi: I will warn on any FFI call.
  • --forbid-unsafe: I will error on unsafe modules.
  • These are my graduated safety levels.

docs.title: Phase 4: Module-Qualified Calls
docs.text:
• I will fix my typechecker for Module.function() syntax.
  • I will provide proper namespace resolution.
  • I will enable explicit module prefixes.

docs.title: My Resources
docs.text:
• docs/MODULE_ARCHITECTURE_DECISION.md - My full architectural decision.
  • docs/MODULE_SYSTEM_REDESIGN.md - My complete redesign specification.
  • docs/MODULE_PHASE1_COMPLETE.md - My Phase 1 implementation summary.
  • docs/MODULE_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md - My phased implementation plan.
  • MEMORY.md - My updated module syntax reference.

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
Questions? Check my docs above or: - Review my examples in examples/sdl_*.nano. - Compare my before and after states in git history: git show 23354ad. - File issues for any migration problems you encounter.

My Migration Status: COMPLETE Phase 1: Production Ready Date: 2025-01-08

docs.title: My Module System
docs.text: I use a module system to organize code, manage dependencies, and bridge the gap between my safe world and the C world. This document describes how I build modules, compile C sources automatically, and handle linking.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
My modules are self-contained packages. They can include: - My source code (.nano files) - C FFI implementations (.c files) - Module metadata (module.json)

When you import a module, I automatically: 1. Compile any C sources if they have changed. 2. Cache the compiled objects in a local build directory. 3. Track dependencies between modules. 4. Generate the correct link commands for the final binary.

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
I expect a typical module directory to look like this:

modules/sdl_ttf/
├── module.json              # Module metadata (required if C sources exist)
├── sdl_ttf.nano             # Main module file (required)
├── sdl_ttf_helpers.c        # C implementation (optional)
└── sdl_ttf_helpers.nano     # Additional nanolang helpers (optional)

docs.title: module.json Specification
docs.text: I use the module.json file to understand how to build and link a module.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
{
  "name": "module_name",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Short description of the module",

  "c_sources": [
    "helper.c",
    "another_file.c"
  ],

  "system_libs": [
    "SDL2_ttf",
    "m"
  ],

  "pkg_config": [
    "sdl2_ttf"
  ],

  "include_dirs": [
    "/usr/local/include"
  ],

  "cflags": [
    "-O2",
    "-Wall"
  ],

  "ldflags": [
    "-L/usr/local/lib"
  ],

  "dependencies": [
    "sdl"
  ]
}

docs.title: Required Fields
docs.text: • name (string): The module name. This should match the directory name.

docs.title: Optional Fields
docs.text:
• version (string): Semantic version (e.g., "1.0.0").
  • description (string): A brief description of what the module does.
  • c_sources (array of strings): C source files I should compile. These are relative to the module directory.
  • system_libs (array of strings): System libraries I need to link (e.g., "SDL2", "m", "pthread").
  • pkg_config (array of strings): pkg-config packages I should use for compile and link flags.
  • include_dirs (array of strings): Additional directories I should search for headers.
  • cflags (array of strings): Additional compiler flags I should pass to the C compiler.
  • ldflags (array of strings): Additional linker flags I should use.
  • dependencies (array of strings): Other modules this module depends on.

docs.title: Field Priority
docs.text:
When I determine build flags, I apply this priority. Later items override earlier ones:

  1. Default system flags
  2. pkg_config flags
  3. include_dirs -> -I flags
  4. cflags (custom compile flags)
  5. ldflags (custom link flags)
  6. system_libs -> -l flags

docs.title: When I Process an Import
docs.text:
1. I parse module.json if it exists in the module directory.
  2. I check dependencies and recursively process any dependent modules.
  3. I check my build cache:
  4. Module build directory: modules/module_name/.build/
  5. Object file: modules/module_name/.build/module_name.o
  6. Metadata: modules/module_name/.build/.build_info.json
  7. I rebuild if necessary:
  8. I find a C source that is newer than the object file.
  9. The module.json file has changed.
  10. A dependency has been rebuilt.
  11. I track the results for linking:
  12. I add the object file to my link list.
  13. I add system_libs to my link list.
  14. I add ldflags to my link list.

docs.title: Build Cache
docs.text:
I create a .build/ directory for each module that contains C sources:

modules/sdl_ttf/
├── .build/
│   ├── sdl_ttf.o              # Compiled object file
│   ├── .build_info.json       # Build metadata
│   └── .compile_commands.json # Compile commands (optional)

.build_info.json: 

{
  "sources": {
    "sdl_ttf_helpers.c": {
      "mtime": 1234567890,
      "size": 12345
    }
  },
  "module_json_mtime": 1234567890,
  "compile_command": "gcc ...",
  "timestamp": "2025-11-17T12:00:00Z"
}

docs.title: Incremental Compilation
docs.text: I only recompile when I must. Specifically: - A source file has been modified (mtime or size changed). - The module.json has been modified. - The object file is missing. - A dependency was rebuilt.

docs.title: Parallel Builds
docs.text: I do not yet compile modules in parallel. This is a planned enhancement for when modules have no interdependencies.

docs.title: Pure Modules
docs.text:
If a module contains no C code, you do not need a module.json file. You can simply import it:

import "modules/my_pure_module/my_module.nano"

docs.title: Modules with C FFI
docs.text:
I handle compilation and linking automatically when you import a module with C code:

import "modules/sdl_ttf/sdl_ttf.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    (TTF_Init)  # C function automatically available
    return 0
}

When you compile: 

nanoc my_app.nano -o my_app

I perform these steps: 1. I detect the sdl_ttf module import. 2. I read modules/sdl_ttf/module.json. 3. I compile sdl_ttf_helpers.c if my cache indicates it is stale. 4. I link with the SDL2_ttf system library. 5. I produce the final binary.

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text:
If module A depends on module B, I handle the ordering.

modules/my_module/module.json: 

{
  "name": "my_module",
  "dependencies": ["sdl", "sdl_helpers"],
  "c_sources": ["my_module.c"]
}

When you import my_module, I automatically: 1. Process the sdl module first. 2. Process the sdl_helpers module. 3. Process my_module. 4. Link the object files from all three modules.

docs.title: NANO_MODULE_PATH
docs.text:
I use this semicolon-separated list of directories to search for modules:

export NANO_MODULE_PATH="/usr/local/lib/nano/modules:./modules:~/nano/modules"

If you do not set this, I default to modules relative to the current directory.

docs.title: NANO_BUILD_CACHE
docs.text:
I use this directory for my module build cache:

export NANO_BUILD_CACHE="/tmp/nano_build_cache"

If you do not set this, I default to .build/ within each module directory.

docs.title: NANO_CC
docs.text:
I use this C compiler:

export NANO_CC=clang

I default to gcc if you do not specify otherwise.

docs.title: NANO_VERBOSE_BUILD
docs.text:
If you enable this, I will provide verbose output during the build process:

export NANO_VERBOSE_BUILD=1
nanoc my_app.nano -o my_app

docs.title: Example 1: SDL Window Module
docs.text:
modules/sdl/module.json: 

{
  "name": "sdl",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "SDL2 windowing, rendering, and events",
  "pkg_config": ["sdl2"]
}

This module has no C sources. I use the system SDL2 library directly.

docs.title: Example 2: SDL Helpers with C Code
docs.text:
modules/sdl_helpers/module.json: 

{
  "name": "sdl_helpers",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Helper functions for SDL rendering",
  "c_sources": ["sdl_helpers.c"],
  "pkg_config": ["sdl2"],
  "dependencies": ["sdl"]
}

This module has a C implementation and depends on my sdl module.

docs.title: Example 3: SDL_ttf Extension
docs.text:
modules/sdl_ttf/module.json: 

{
  "name": "sdl_ttf",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "TrueType font rendering for SDL",
  "c_sources": ["sdl_ttf_helpers.c"],
  "pkg_config": ["sdl2_ttf"],
  "dependencies": ["sdl"]
}

docs.title: "pkg-config: command not found"
docs.text: I need pkg-config to find system libraries. You should install it: - macOS: brew install pkg-config - Linux: sudo apt-get install pkg-config

docs.title: "No package 'sdl2_ttf' found"
docs.text: I cannot find the development files for this library. You should install the development package: - macOS: brew install sdl2_ttf - Linux: sudo apt-get install libsdl2-ttf-dev

docs.title: Module not rebuilding
docs.text:
If you think I should rebuild a module but I am not doing so, you can clear my build cache: 

rm -rf modules/*/build/

Alternatively, you can update the timestamp on the source file: 

touch modules/sdl_ttf/sdl_ttf_helpers.c

docs.title: Verbose build output
docs.text:
If you want to see exactly what I am doing during a build, set this environment variable:

NANO_VERBOSE_BUILD=1 nanoc my_app.nano -o my_app

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Keep module.json minimal. Only specify what I need to know.
  2. Use pkg-config when available. This is more reliable than hardcoded paths.
  3. Version your modules. I recommend semantic versioning.
  4. Document dependencies. List all other modules your module depends on.
  5. Test incremental builds. Ensure my cache works as you expect.
  6. Provide examples. Show others how to use your module.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• [ ] Parallel module compilation
  • [ ] Cross-compilation support
  • [ ] Module registry and package manager
  • [ ] Automatic dependency installation
  • [ ] Binary module distribution (.a/.so files)
  • [ ] Module versioning and compatibility checking

docs.title: Module Systems Comparison: Python, Go, Ruby, Elixir vs NanoLang
docs.text: Purpose: Understand what makes module systems feel "first-class" Date: 2025-01-08

docs.title: What Makes a Module System Feel "First-Class"?
Key Characteristics
docs.text:
1. Modules are values - Can be passed, stored, inspected
  2. Introspection built-in - List exports, query metadata
  3. Clear safety/trust model - Know what's safe vs unsafe
  4. Namespace control - Explicit imports, no pollution
  5. Tooling support - Docs, linters, analyzers can query modules

docs.title: Import Syntax
docs.text:
import math                    # Import entire module
from math import sqrt, sin     # Import specific functions
import numpy as np             # Alias
from os import *               # Import all (discouraged)

docs.title: Module as First-Class Object
docs.text:
import math

# Module IS an object
type(math)  # <class 'module'>

# Access as attribute
math.sqrt(16)  # 4.0

# Introspection
dir(math)  # ['__doc__', '__name__', 'sqrt', 'sin', 'cos', ...]
math.__name__  # 'math'
math.__file__  # '/usr/lib/python3.9/lib-dynload/math.cpython-39.so'
math.__dict__  # Full namespace dictionary

# Check if function exists
hasattr(math, 'sqrt')  # True
hasattr(math, 'foo')   # False

# Get function dynamically
getattr(math, 'sqrt')  # <built-in function sqrt>

docs.title: Inspection Module
docs.text:
import inspect
import math

# Get all functions
funcs = inspect.getmembers(math, inspect.isfunction)
# [('sqrt', <built-in>), ('sin', <built-in>), ...]

# Function signature
sig = inspect.signature(math.sqrt)
print(sig)  # (x, /)

# Source code (for pure Python modules)
import json
source = inspect.getsource(json.loads)
print(source)  # Full function source

docs.title: Safety Model
docs.text:
Problem: Python has no built-in module safety markers

import ctypes  # Unsafe! Can crash Python
import requests  # Safe HTTP library

# No way to tell which is safe without reading docs

Community Solution: Type hints + mypy

from typing import List, Dict

def safe_function(items: List[str]) -> Dict[str, int]:
    # Type checker verifies safety
    pass

docs.title: Import Syntax
docs.text:
package main

import (
    "fmt"              // Standard library
    "math"
    "unsafe"           // Explicitly unsafe!
    "github.com/user/repo"  // Remote package
)

docs.title: Package Metadata
docs.text:
package mypackage

// Package-level documentation
// This appears in godoc

// Exported (capitalized)
func PublicFunction() {
    privateFunction()  // Can call private
}

// Unexported (lowercase)
func privateFunction() {
    // Only visible within package
}

docs.title: Introspection via Reflection
docs.text:
import "reflect"

// Get type information
t := reflect.TypeOf(math.Sqrt)
fmt.Println(t)  // func(float64) float64

// List package functions (compile-time via go doc)
// $ go doc math
// package math -- go/doc
// func Sqrt(x float64) float64
// func Sin(x float64) float64
// ...

docs.title: Safety Model: unsafe Package
docs.text:
Explicit Unsafe Import: 

import "unsafe"

// Using unsafe is VISUALLY OBVIOUS
ptr := unsafe.Pointer(&x)

Compile-Time Checks: 

# Lint for unsafe usage
$ go vet ./...
# Flag unsafe packages
$ grep -r "import unsafe" .

Why It Works: - ✅ One import line makes entire usage visible - ✅ No unsafe {} blocks scattered everywhere - ✅ Easy to audit: search for "import unsafe" - ✅ Tooling can flag unsafe packages

docs.title: Import Syntax
docs.text:
require 'json'           # Load library
require_relative 'my_module'  # Load local file

docs.title: Module as Object
docs.text:
module Vector2D
  def self.add(v1, v2)
    [v1[0] + v2[0], v1[1] + v2[1]]
  end

  def self.magnitude(v)
    Math.sqrt(v[0]**2 + v[1]**2)
  end
end

# Module IS an object
Vector2D.class  # Module

# List methods
Vector2D.methods(false)  # [:add, :magnitude]

# Check method exists
Vector2D.respond_to?(:add)  # true

# Call dynamically
Vector2D.send(:add, [1,2], [3,4])  # [4, 6]

docs.title: Introspection
docs.text:
module MyModule
  @@version = "1.0.0"
  @@safe = true

  def self.version
    @@version
  end

  def self.safe?
    @@safe
  end
end

# Custom metadata
MyModule.version  # "1.0.0"
MyModule.safe?    # true

# List instance variables
MyModule.instance_variables  # [:@@version, :@@safe]

# Module hierarchy
MyModule.ancestors  # [MyModule]

docs.title: Refinements (Scoped Monkey-Patching)
docs.text:
module StringExtensions
  refine String do
    def shout
      self.upcase + "!"
    end
  end
end

# Only active when explicitly used
using StringExtensions
"hello".shout  # "HELLO!"

Why It Works: - ✅ Explicit scope control - ✅ No global pollution - ✅ Clear activation point

docs.title: Module Definition
docs.text:
defmodule Vector2D do
  @moduledoc """
  2D vector mathematics module.
  Provides pure functions for vector operations.
  """

  @vsn "1.0.0"
  @safe true
  @author "Elixir Team"

  @doc """
  Adds two 2D vectors.
  """
  def add({x1, y1}, {x2, y2}) do
    {x1 + x2, y1 + y2}
  end

  def magnitude({x, y}) do
    :math.sqrt(x * x + y * y)
  end
end

docs.title: Compile-Time Introspection
docs.text:
# List functions
Vector2D.__info__(:functions)
# [add: 2, magnitude: 1]

# List attributes
Vector2D.__info__(:attributes)
# [vsn: ["1.0.0"], moduledoc: "...", safe: true]

# Module compiled?
Vector2D.__info__(:compile)
# [...compile options...]

# Full module info
Vector2D.module_info()
# [module: Vector2D, exports: [...], attributes: [...]]

docs.title: Custom Attributes for Safety
docs.text:
defmodule UnsafeFFI do
  @unsafe true
  @ffi_calls [:c_malloc, :c_free, :memcpy]

  def allocate(size) do
    # Calls C function
    :c_malloc(size)
  end
end

# Check safety at compile time
if UnsafeFFI.__info__(:attributes)[:unsafe] do
  IO.warn("Using unsafe module UnsafeFFI")
end

docs.title: Macros for Module Generation
docs.text:
defmodule AutoWrap do
  defmacro wrap_unsafe(module_name, ffi_funcs) do
    quote do
      defmodule unquote(module_name) do
        @unsafe true
        @wrapped_ffi unquote(ffi_funcs)

        # Generate safe wrappers automatically
        for {func, arity} <- unquote(ffi_funcs) do
          def unquote(func)(args) do
            # Safe wrapper logic
            apply(:erlang, unquote(func), args)
          end
        end
      end
    end
  end
end

# Usage
AutoWrap.wrap_unsafe(SafeSDL, [
  {:SDL_Init, 1},
  {:SDL_Quit, 0}
])

Why It Works: - ✅ Module attributes as metadata - ✅ Compile-time introspection - ✅ Macro system for code generation - ✅ Clear module boundaries

docs.title: Module System
docs.text:
mod vector2d {
    pub fn add(v1: (f64, f64), v2: (f64, f64)) -> (f64, f64) {
        (v1.0 + v2.0, v1.1 + v2.1)
    }
}

use vector2d::add;

docs.title: Safety Model
docs.text:
// Safe Rust (default)
fn safe_function() {
    let x = vec![1, 2, 3];
    println!("{}", x[0]);  // Bounds-checked
}

// Unsafe Rust (explicit)
fn dangerous_function() {
    unsafe {
        let ptr = 0x12345 as *const i32;
        let val = *ptr;  // Unsafe dereference
    }
}

Why It Works: - ✅ unsafe keyword required for dangerous operations - ✅ Compiler enforces safety boundaries - ✅ Easy to audit: search for unsafe - ❌ BUT: Still requires scattered unsafe {} blocks

What Rust Does Better: - FFI calls in separate extern blocks - Unsafe traits clearly marked

extern "C" {
    fn SDL_Init(flags: u32) -> i32;
}

// All calls to SDL_Init require unsafe
fn init_sdl() {
    unsafe {
        SDL_Init(0);
    }
}

docs.title: Current NanoLang Issues vs Other Languages
Issue 1: No Module Introspection
docs.text:
Python: 

import math
dir(math)  # ✅ Lists all exports

Go: 

go doc math  # ✅ Lists all functions

Ruby: 

Math.methods  # ✅ Lists all methods

Elixir: 

Math.__info__(:functions)  # ✅ Lists all functions

NanoLang (Current): 

import "modules/math_ext/math_ext.nano"
// ❌ NO WAY to list exports
// ❌ NO WAY to check if function exists
// ❌ NO WAY to get metadata

docs.title: Issue 2: Scattered Unsafe Blocks
docs.text:
Go: 

import "unsafe"  // ✅ One line, entire module marked

func do_stuff() {
    ptr := unsafe.Pointer(&x)  // No extra blocks
}

Rust: 

unsafe fn entire_function_unsafe() {
    // ✅ Function-level unsafe
    raw_ptr_operation();
    another_unsafe_call();
}

NanoLang (Current): 

fn render() -> void {
    unsafe { (SDL_Init 0) }      // ❌ Noisy
    unsafe { (SDL_CreateWindow) }  // ❌ Noisy
    unsafe { (SDL_Present) }       // ❌ Noisy
    unsafe { (SDL_Quit) }          // ❌ Noisy
}

What Users Want: 

unsafe module sdl { /* ... */ }

fn render() -> void {
    (SDL_Init 0)       // ✅ Clean
    (SDL_CreateWindow)  // ✅ Clean
    (SDL_Present)       // ✅ Clean
    (SDL_Quit)          // ✅ Clean
}

docs.title: Issue 3: Module Not First-Class
docs.text:
Python: 

import math

def use_module(mod):
    if hasattr(mod, 'sqrt'):
        return mod.sqrt(16)

use_module(math)  # ✅ Modules are values

Elixir: 

defmodule Caller do
  def call_function(module, func, args) do
    apply(module, func, args)  # ✅ Modules are atoms
  end
end

Caller.call_function(Math, :sqrt, [16])

NanoLang (Current): 

import "modules/math_ext/math_ext.nano" as Math

fn use_module(m: ???) -> int {
    // ❌ No Module type
    // ❌ Can't pass modules as values
    return 0
}

docs.title: Issue 4: No Safety Metadata
docs.text:
Go (via tooling): 

$ go-safer check .
Warning: package main imports "unsafe"
  Used in: main.go:45

Python (via type hints): 

from typing import cast, Any

# Type checker warns about unsafe casts
x: int = cast(int, some_object)  # mypy warning

Elixir (via attributes): 

if MyModule.__info__(:attributes)[:unsafe] do
  IO.warn("Using unsafe module")
end

NanoLang (Current): 

import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"

// ❌ No way to check if module is safe
// ❌ No compiler warnings
// ❌ No metadata available

docs.title: Comparison Table
docs.text: Feature Python Go Ruby Elixir NanoLang (Current) NanoLang (Proposed) Module Introspection ✅ dir() ✅ go doc ✅ .methods ✅ __info__ ❌ None ✅ __module_info_* Module as Value ✅ Yes ⚠️ Via reflection ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ❌ No ⏳ Future Safety Annotation ❌ No ✅ import "unsafe" ❌ No ✅ Custom attrs ❌ No ✅ unsafe module Explicit Imports ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes Namespace Control ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ⚠️ Limited ✅ Enhanced Warning System ⚠️ Linters ✅ go vet ⚠️ Linters ⚠️ Custom ❌ No ✅ Compiler flags Function-level Unsafe N/A ❌ Block-level N/A N/A ❌ Block-level ✅ Module-level Auto-documentation ✅ pydoc ✅ godoc ✅ rdoc ✅ ExDoc ❌ No ✅ Via metadata Compile-time Metadata ❌ Runtime only ✅ Yes ❌ Runtime only ✅ Yes ❌ No ✅ Yes

docs.title: What Makes Go's unsafe Work
docs.text:
1. One import line - import "unsafe" makes entire usage obvious
  2. Tooling integration - go vet flags unsafe usage
  3. No ceremony - Don't need unsafe {} for every call
  4. Clear audit trail - Search for one string

docs.title: What Makes Python's Introspection Work
docs.text:
1. Modules are objects - Can inspect like any other object
  2. dir() everywhere - Universal introspection protocol
  3. __dict__ access - Full namespace visibility
  4. inspect module - Rich metadata access

docs.title: What Makes Elixir's Attributes Work
docs.text:
1. Compile-time metadata - @moduledoc, @vsn, custom attrs
  2. __info__/1 protocol - Standard introspection interface
  3. Macro system - Generate metadata automatically
  4. Clear module boundaries - defmodule wraps everything

docs.title: Design Goals for NanoLang
docs.text: Based on this comparison, NanoLang should have:

docs.title: 1. Module-Level Safety (Like Go)
docs.text:
unsafe module sdl {
    extern fn SDL_Init(flags: int) -> int
    // No unsafe blocks needed inside
}

docs.title: 2. Compile-Time Introspection (Like Elixir)
docs.text:
let info: ModuleInfo = (__module_info_sdl)
assert (not info.is_safe)
assert info.has_ffi

docs.title: 3. Warning System (Like Go vet)
docs.text:
nanoc app.nano --warn-unsafe-imports
nanoc app.nano --warn-ffi
nanoc app.nano --forbid-unsafe

docs.title: 4. Module Metadata (Like Python + Elixir)
docs.text:
struct ModuleInfo {
    name: string,
    version: string,
    is_safe: bool,
    has_ffi: bool,
    exported_functions: array<FunctionInfo>
}

docs.title: 5. Clear Import Syntax (Like All)
docs.text:
import safe "modules/vector2d/vector2d.nano"
import unsafe "modules/sdl/sdl.nano"

docs.title: For Users
docs.text:
• Less visual noise - One unsafe module vs dozens of unsafe {} blocks
  • Clear safety model - Know what's safe at a glance
  • Better tooling - Docs, linters, analyzers can query modules
  • Easier auditing - --warn-unsafe-imports shows all risks

docs.title: For Language
docs.text:
• Competitive feature - Matches Python/Go/Elixir/Rust
  • Foundation for ecosystem - Module marketplace, certifications
  • Better error messages - "Function from unsafe module 'sdl'"
  • Metaprogramming support - Generate code based on module metadata

docs.title: For Ecosystem
docs.text:
• Module ratings - Safety scores in package registry
  • Automated audits - Tools can scan for unsafe usage
  • Dependency analysis - See safety impact of all dependencies
  • Documentation generation - Auto-generate from module metadata

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Read: docs/MODULE_SYSTEM_REDESIGN.md - Full proposal
  2. Decide: Module-level safety annotations?
  3. Decide: Module introspection system?
  4. Decide: Warning flags?
  5. Implement: Phase 1 (safety annotations) - 1-2 weeks

Status: 🔴 Research Complete - Ready for architectural decision Date: 2025-01-08 Related: docs/MODULE_SYSTEM_REDESIGN.md

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: I now support complete multi-file project development. I handle project-relative imports, module caching, constant sharing, and automatic dependency resolution.

docs.title: Project-Relative Imports
docs.text:
You can import files from anywhere in your project using project-relative paths.

// Import from examples directory
import "examples/myproject/types.nano"
import "examples/myproject/utils.nano"

// Import from src directory
import "src/core/parser.nano"
import "src/core/lexer.nano"

My compiler automatically performs these steps: 1. I detect imports starting with examples/ or src/. 2. I walk up the directory tree to find the project root. 3. I resolve paths relative to the project root. 4. I work regardless of where you invoke compilation.

docs.title: Module Caching and Deduplication
docs.text:
I prevent duplicate imports and circular dependencies.

// types.nano - base types
enum Status { Ok, Error }

// parser.nano
import "examples/myproject/types.nano"  // First load

// main.nano  
import "examples/myproject/types.nano"  // Cached, skips
import "examples/myproject/parser.nano" // Loads, types already cached

My Cache Lifecycle: - I clear the cache at the start of compilation. - I cache modules as I load them during import processing. - Each .nano to .o compilation gets an isolated cache. - I do this to prevent "already defined" errors.

docs.title: Constant Export and Inlining
docs.text:
I automatically export and inline your top-level immutable constants.

// config.nano
let MAX_CONNECTIONS: int = 100
let PI: float = 3.14159
let DEBUG: bool = true

// server.nano
import "examples/myproject/config.nano"

fn init_server() -> int {
    let pool_size = MAX_CONNECTIONS * 2  // Constant inlined!
    // ...
}

I transpile this to: 

int64_t pool_size = (100 * 2LL);  // Constant value inlined

docs.title: Recursive Import Processing
docs.text:
My modules can import other modules transitively.

types.nano (base types)
  ↑
parser.nano (imports types)
  ↑
compiler.nano (imports parser, gets types transitively)

docs.title: Example: Project Structure
docs.text:
This is how I expect a complete multi-file project to look.

examples/myproject/
├── types.nano     - Base types, enums, constants
├── utils.nano     - Helper functions
├── core.nano      - Core logic
├── main.nano      - Entry point
├── Makefile
└── README.md

Import Structure: 

// types.nano - Base definitions
enum Status { Ok, Error, Pending }
let MAX_RETRIES: int = 3
let TIMEOUT: float = 30.0

// utils.nano - Uses types
import "examples/myproject/types.nano"
let retry_count = MAX_RETRIES * 2  // Constants work!

// core.nano - Uses types and utils
import "examples/myproject/types.nano"
import "examples/myproject/utils.nano"

// main.nano - Uses everything
import "examples/myproject/types.nano"
import "examples/myproject/utils.nano"
import "examples/myproject/core.nano"

Compilation: 

cd examples/myproject
make
# Compiles main.nano with all imports
# Each module compiled to .o file
# Linked together

docs.title: Generic Module System
docs.text: I use metadata-driven header inclusion for my modules.

docs.title: Module Schema (module.json)
docs.text:
{
  "name": "sdl_mixer",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Audio mixing for SDL",
  "headers": ["SDL_mixer.h"],          // ← C headers to include
  "c_sources": [],
  "pkg_config": ["SDL2_mixer"],        // ← Compile/link flags
  "dependencies": ["sdl"]              // ← Module dependencies
}

docs.title: Supported Libraries
docs.text:
I can wrap any C library.

SDL Ecosystem: - SDL2: "headers": ["SDL.h"] - SDL2_mixer: "headers": ["SDL_mixer.h"] - SDL2_ttf: "headers": ["SDL_ttf.h"] - SDL2_image: "headers": ["SDL_image.h"]

Graphics: - CUDA: "headers": ["cuda.h", "cuda_runtime.h"] - OpenGL: "headers": ["GL/gl.h"] - Vulkan: "headers": ["vulkan/vulkan.h"]

UI Frameworks: - GTK: "headers": ["gtk/gtk.h"], "pkg_config": ["gtk+-3.0"] - Qt: Custom headers and flags

Utilities: - curl: "headers": ["curl/curl.h"] - zlib: "headers": ["zlib.h"] - libpng: "headers": ["png.h"]

docs.title: 1. Module Resolution (module.c)
docs.text:
char *resolve_module_path(const char *module_path, const char *current_file) {
    // Detect project-relative paths
    if (strncmp(module_path, "examples/", 9) == 0 || 
        strncmp(module_path, "src/", 4) == 0) {
        // Walk up directory tree to find project root
        // Resolve path relative to root
    }
    // ...
}

docs.title: 2. Module Cache
docs.text:
typedef struct {
    char **loaded_paths;
    int count;
    int capacity;
} ModuleCache;

// Global cache cleared at compilation start
static ModuleCache *module_cache = NULL;

docs.title: 3. Constant Evaluation
docs.text:
// In process_imports() - module.c
if (module_item->type == AST_LET && !module_item->as.let.is_mut) {
    // Evaluate literal values
    if (value_node->type == AST_NUMBER) {
        val = create_int(value_node->as.number);
    }
    // Store in environment
    env_define_var(env, name, type, false, val);
}

docs.title: 4. Constant Inlining
docs.text:
// In transpile_expression() - transpiler.c
case AST_IDENTIFIER: {
    // Check if immutable constant
    if (var_index >= 0 && !env->symbols[var_index].is_mut) {
        if (val.type == VAL_INT) {
            // Inline the literal value
            snprintf(num_buf, sizeof(num_buf), "%lld", val.as.int_val);
            sb_append(sb, num_buf);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text: Before After Single-file only Multi-file projects No code reuse Shared constants and types Monolithic programs Modular architecture Hardcoded SDL Generic library support

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
1. Module Visibility
  2. I will add public and private exports.

  3. I will support selective imports: import { function_name } from "module".

  4. Package Management

  5. I plan to have a package registry.
  6. I will support semantic versioning.

  7. I will handle dependency resolution.

  8. Expression Evaluation

  9. I will support compile-time computed constants.

  10. I will implement constant folding optimization.

  11. Type Exports

  12. I will allow sharing of struct, enum, and union definitions.
  13. I will support type aliases across modules.

docs.title: Example: Creating a CUDA Module
docs.text:
// modules/cuda/module.json
{
  "name": "cuda",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "NVIDIA CUDA GPU computing",
  "headers": [
    "cuda.h",
    "cuda_runtime.h"
  ],
  "c_sources": ["cuda_helpers.c"],
  "system_libs": ["cuda", "cudart"],
  "include_dirs": ["/usr/local/cuda/include"],
  "ldflags": ["-L/usr/local/cuda/lib64"]
}

// modules/cuda/cuda.nano
extern fn cudaMalloc(ptr: int, size: int) -> int
extern fn cudaMemcpy(dst: int, src: int, size: int, kind: int) -> int
extern fn cudaFree(ptr: int) -> int

// Usage in your program
import "modules/cuda/cuda.nano"

fn main() -> int {
    let device_ptr: int = 0
    cudaMalloc(device_ptr, 1024)
    // ...
}

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
My support for multi-file projects enables: - Professional software architecture. - Large-scale application development. - Team collaboration on codebases. - Reusable module libraries. - Integration with any C library.

My future for development is clear.

docs.title: My Namespace and Module System
docs.text: This is my guide to modules, namespaces, imports, and exports. I describe how I organize code and how I manage what is visible between files.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Module Declaration
  3. Import Syntax
  4. Export Control (pub)
  5. Qualified Access
  6. Module Structure
  7. Best Practices
  8. Examples

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: My module system provides: - Explicit imports - I require you to import only what you need. - Namespace control - I distinguish between public and private symbols. - Path-based modules - I use a file-based module system. - Selective imports - I allow importing specific symbols or entire modules. - Aliasing - I let you rename imports to avoid conflicts.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Module: A .nano file that I can import.
  • Namespace: I declare these via the module keyword.
  • Public symbols: I mark these with pub. They are accessible to importers.
  • Private symbols: I treat symbols without pub as private to the module.
  • Import: I bring external symbols into the current scope.

docs.title: Module Declaration
docs.text:
I let you declare a module namespace. This is optional, but I recommend it for larger projects.

module my_app

# Your code here

Benefits: - I prevent symbol collisions. - I make module boundaries explicit. - I enable better code organization.

Note: My module declaration is optional. I treat files without it as valid modules.

docs.title: Selective Import (Recommended)
docs.text:
I let you import specific symbols from a module.

from "modules/readline/readline.nano" import rl_readline, rl_add_history

Syntax: 

from "path/to/module.nano" import symbol1, symbol2, symbol3

Use when: - I only need to see a few functions. - You want to keep your imports explicit. - You want to avoid polluting my namespace.

docs.title: Import All (Use Sparingly)
docs.text:
I can import all public symbols from a module.

import "path/to/module.nano"

Caution: This brings all public symbols into my namespace. It can cause name conflicts.

docs.title: Import with Alias
docs.text:
I allow importing and renaming to avoid conflicts.

from "modules/math_ext.nano" import sqrt as math_sqrt
from "modules/graphics.nano" import sqrt as gfx_sqrt

I also allow aliasing the entire module.

import "modules/std/io/stdio.nano" as io

# Use qualified names:
let file: int = (io.fopen "test.txt" "r")

docs.title: Export Control (pub)
docs.text: I control what is visible to importers using the pub keyword.

docs.title: Public Functions
docs.text:
# Exported - visible to importers
pub fn exported_function(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x 2)
}

# Private - module-only
fn internal_helper(x: int) -> int {
    return (+ x 1)
}

docs.title: Public Types
docs.text:
# Exported struct
pub struct Config {
    enabled: bool,
    timeout: int
}

# Private struct
struct InternalState {
    counter: int
}

docs.title: 1. Be Explicit with Imports
docs.text:
**Good: 

from "utils.nano" import add, multiply, divide

**Avoid: 

import "utils.nano"  # Imports everything

docs.title: 2. Use Public Sparingly
docs.text:
I only expose what consumers need.

pub fn api_function() -> int { ... }
fn internal_helper() -> int { ... }  # Private

docs.title: 3. Group Related Imports
docs.text:
# Standard library
from "modules/std/io/stdio.nano" import fopen, fclose
from "modules/std/collections/list.nano" import List_new

# Third-party modules
from "modules/json/json.nano" import parse, stringify

# Local modules
from "./types.nano" import Config
from "./utils.nano" import helper

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• MODULE_SYSTEM.md - My module build system and module.json.
  • EXTERN_FFI.md - My C FFI and external functions.
  • QUICK_REFERENCE.md - My syntax cheat sheet.
  • SPECIFICATION.md - My complete language specification.

Last Updated: February 20, 2026 Status: Complete Version: 0.2.0+

docs.title: NanoISA Virtual Machine Architecture
docs.text: I provide a complete virtual machine backend as an alternative to C transpilation. I compile my source code to a custom bytecode format (.nvm) and execute it in a stack-based virtual machine with process-isolated FFI.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
.nano source
  |
  v
nano_virt (compiler)
  |
  +---> .nvm bytecode file (--emit-nvm)
  |       |
  |       v
  |     nano_vm (VM executor)
  |       |
  |       +---> nano_cop (FFI co-process, on demand)
  |       +---> nano_vmd (VM daemon, optional)
  |
  +---> native binary (default)
          embeds .nvm + VM runtime

docs.title: Binaries
docs.text: Binary Description nano_virt My compiler: .nano source to .nvm bytecode or native binary nano_vm My VM executor: loads and runs .nvm files nano_cop My FFI co-process: isolates external function calls nano_vmd My VM daemon: persistent VM process for reduced startup latency

docs.title: nano_virt (Compiler)
docs.text:
nano_virt input.nano [-o output] [--run] [--emit-nvm] [--daemon-wrapper] [-v]
  • -o <path>: Output file (native binary or .nvm)
  • --run: Execute immediately after compilation (in-process VM)
  • --emit-nvm: Write raw .nvm bytecode instead of native binary
  • --daemon-wrapper: Generate thin daemon-mode binary (requires nano_vmd)
  • -v: Verbose output

docs.title: nano_vm (Executor)
docs.text:
nano_vm [--daemon] [--isolate-ffi] <file.nvm>
  • --daemon: Send .nvm to nano_vmd daemon (lazy-launches if needed)
  • --isolate-ffi: Route FFI calls through nano_cop co-process

docs.title: nano_vmd (Daemon)
docs.text: My daemon listens on a Unix domain socket (/tmp/nanolang_vm_<uid>.sock) and accepts .nvm blobs from clients. I use this to reduce startup cost when repeatedly running programs.

docs.title: Architecture Model
docs.text:
• Stack machine with 5 virtual registers: SP (stack pointer), FP (frame pointer), IP (instruction pointer), R0 (accumulator), R1 (scratch)
  • Runtime-typed values: 16 bytes per value (1-byte tag + 15-byte payload)
  • Variable-length encoding: 1-byte opcode + 0-4 operands
  • Little-endian byte order

docs.title: Value Types
docs.text: Tag Code Description TAG_VOID 0x00 No value TAG_INT 0x01 64-bit signed integer TAG_U8 0x02 Unsigned byte TAG_FLOAT 0x03 64-bit IEEE 754 double TAG_BOOL 0x04 true/false TAG_STRING 0x05 Heap-allocated, GC-managed, immutable TAG_BSTRING 0x06 Binary string with length TAG_ARRAY 0x07 Dynamic array, GC-managed TAG_STRUCT 0x08 Named struct with fields TAG_ENUM 0x09 Integer variant index TAG_UNION 0x0A Tagged union TAG_FUNCTION 0x0B Function table index + optional closure env TAG_TUPLE 0x0C Fixed-size heterogeneous container TAG_HASHMAP 0x0D Key-value map TAG_OPAQUE 0x0E RPC proxy ID (handle to co-process object)

docs.title: Opcodes (178 total)
docs.text:
Stack & Constants (0x00-0x0F): NOP, PUSH_I64, PUSH_F64, PUSH_BOOL, PUSH_STR, PUSH_VOID, PUSH_U8, DUP, POP, SWAP, ROT3

Variable Access (0x10-0x1F): LOAD_LOCAL, STORE_LOCAL (FP-relative), LOAD_GLOBAL, STORE_GLOBAL, LOAD_UPVALUE, STORE_UPVALUE (closure captures)

Arithmetic (0x20-0x27): ADD (also string concatenation), SUB, MUL, DIV (0 on div-by-zero), MOD, NEG

Comparison (0x28-0x2F): EQ, NE, LT, LE, GT, GE

Logic (0x30-0x37): AND, OR, NOT

Control Flow (0x38-0x3F): JMP (relative i32), JMP_TRUE, JMP_FALSE, CALL, CALL_INDIRECT, CALL_EXTERN (FFI via RPC), CALL_MODULE (cross-module), RET

String Ops (0x40-0x4F): STR_LEN, STR_CONCAT, STR_SUBSTR, STR_CONTAINS, STR_EQ, STR_CHAR_AT, STR_FROM_INT, STR_FROM_FLOAT

Array Ops (0x50-0x5F): ARR_NEW, ARR_PUSH, ARR_POP, ARR_GET, ARR_SET, ARR_LEN, ARR_SLICE, ARR_REMOVE, ARR_LITERAL

Struct Ops (0x60-0x67): STRUCT_NEW, STRUCT_GET, STRUCT_SET, STRUCT_LITERAL

Union/Enum Ops (0x68-0x6F): UNION_CONSTRUCT, UNION_TAG, UNION_FIELD, MATCH_TAG, ENUM_VAL

Tuple Ops (0x70-0x77): TUPLE_NEW, TUPLE_GET

Hashmap Ops (0x78-0x7F): HM_NEW, HM_GET, HM_SET, HM_HAS, HM_DELETE, HM_KEYS, HM_VALUES, HM_LEN

GC/Memory (0x80-0x87): GC_RETAIN, GC_RELEASE, GC_SCOPE_ENTER, GC_SCOPE_EXIT

Type Casts (0x88-0x8F): CAST_INT, CAST_FLOAT, CAST_BOOL, CAST_STRING, TYPE_CHECK

Closures (0x90-0x97): CLOSURE_NEW (fn_idx + capture_count), CLOSURE_CALL

I/O & Debug (0xA0-0xAF): PRINT, ASSERT, DEBUG_LINE, HALT

Opaque Proxy (0xB0-0xBF): OPAQUE_NULL, OPAQUE_VALID

docs.title: Header (32 bytes)
docs.text:
[magic: "NVM\x01" (4B)] [version (4B)] [flags (4B)] [entry_point (4B)]
[section_count (4B)] [string_pool_offset (4B)] [string_pool_length (4B)] [CRC32 checksum (4B)]

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
Each section has a 12-byte directory entry: [type (4B)] [offset (4B)] [size (4B)]

Section Type Contents CODE 0x0001 Bytecode instructions STRINGS 0x0002 String constant pool (deduplicated) FUNCTIONS 0x0003 Function table (name, arity, code offset, locals, upvalues) STRUCTS 0x0004 Struct type definitions ENUMS 0x0005 Enum type definitions UNIONS 0x0006 Union type definitions GLOBALS 0x0007 Global variable declarations IMPORTS 0x0008 Extern function stubs for FFI DEBUG 0x0009 Source maps (bytecode offset to source line) METADATA 0x000A Module name, version MODULE_REFS 0x000B Referenced module names for cross-module linking

docs.title: String Pool
docs.text: Variable-length entries: [length: u32] [utf8_bytes: length]. I deduplicate strings at compile time.

docs.title: Function Entries (18 bytes each)
docs.text: [name_idx (4B)] [arity (2B)] [code_offset (4B)] [code_length (4B)] [local_count (2B)] [upvalue_count (2B)]

docs.title: Trap Architecture
docs.text:
My VM separates pure computation from side effects.

My pure core (vm_core_execute) handles 83+ opcodes: - All arithmetic, logic, comparison - Stack manipulation, variable access - Data structure operations - Control flow (jumps, calls, returns)

When my core encounters a side-effecting operation, it returns a trap descriptor:

Trap Trigger Handler Action TRAP_EXTERN_CALL OP_CALL_EXTERN Route to co-process FFI TRAP_PRINT OP_PRINT Write to stdout TRAP_ASSERT OP_ASSERT Check boolean, abort if false TRAP_HALT OP_HALT Stop execution TRAP_ERROR Runtime error Report and terminate

My harness (vm_execute) dispatches traps and resumes the core. I chose this separation to enable potential FPGA implementation of the pure-compute core.

docs.title: Memory Management
docs.text:
I use reference-counted GC with scope-based auto-release: - OP_GC_RETAIN / OP_GC_RELEASE - Manual reference counting - OP_GC_SCOPE_ENTER / OP_GC_SCOPE_EXIT - Automatic release on scope exit

I insert scope markers for let-bindings at compile time.

docs.title: Call Frames
docs.text: Each function call I execute pushes a frame with: - Function index and return address - Stack base (where locals begin) - Local count (including parameters) - Closure environment (if closure call) - Module reference (for cross-module calls)

docs.title: Co-Process FFI Protocol
docs.text: I isolate external function calls in a separate nano_cop process. I communicate over pipes using a binary protocol.

docs.title: Wire Format
docs.text: 8-byte header: [version (1B)] [msg_type (1B)] [reserved (2B)] [payload_len (4B)]

docs.title: Message Types
docs.text:
VM to Co-Process: - COP_MSG_INIT (0x01) - I send the import table - COP_MSG_FFI_REQ (0x02) - I call an external function (import index + serialized args) - COP_MSG_SHUTDOWN (0x03) - I terminate the process

Co-Process to VM: - COP_MSG_FFI_RESULT (0x10) - Serialized return value - COP_MSG_FFI_ERROR (0x11) - Error string - COP_MSG_READY (0x12) - Init complete

docs.title: Value Serialization
docs.text: Type Encoding INT i64 (8 bytes, little-endian) FLOAT f64 (8 bytes, IEEE 754) BOOL u8 (0 or 1) STRING length (u32) + UTF-8 data ARRAY elem_type (u8) + count (u32) + serialized elements OPAQUE i64 proxy ID VOID 0 bytes

docs.title: Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. I fork a nano_cop subprocess connected via pipes
  2. I send COP_MSG_INIT with the import table
  3. My co-process loads FFI modules and sends COP_MSG_READY
  4. For each FFI call: I send COP_MSG_FFI_REQ, and the co-process sends the result
  5. I send COP_MSG_SHUTDOWN on exit

If my co-process crashes, I detect the broken pipe and recover. I isolate FFI crashes from my execution.

docs.title: Compiler Backend (Codegen)
docs.text:
My nano_virt compiler translates my AST to NanoISA bytecode in three passes.

Pass 1 - Type Registration: I register all struct, enum, union definitions. I register function signatures and globals.

Pass 1.5 - Global Initialization: I compile the __init__ function for global variable initializers.

Pass 2 - Function Bodies: I use two sub-passes per function: - 2a: I scan for loop/branch labels - 2b: I generate bytecode with forward jump patching

docs.title: Native Binary Generation
docs.text:
My wrapper generator (wrapper_gen.c) produces standalone native executables:

  1. Full wrapper (default): I embed .nvm bytecode and link my full VM runtime. I support all features including closures, cross-module calls, and FFI.
  2. Daemon wrapper (--daemon-wrapper): I create a thin binary that connects to my nano_vmd daemon. This footprint is smaller but requires the daemon to be running.

docs.title: NanoISA (src/nanoisa/)
docs.text: File Lines Purpose isa.h / isa.c 401 My instruction set definition, encode/decode nvm_format.h / nvm_format.c 618 My binary format serialization, CRC32 assembler.h / assembler.c 736 My two-pass text assembler disassembler.h / disassembler.c 246 My binary to text with label reconstruction

docs.title: NanoVM (src/nanovm/)
docs.text: File Lines Purpose vm.h / vm.c 1,844 My core switch-dispatch interpreter value.h / value.c 225 My NanoValue constructors, type checking heap.h / heap.c 595 My reference-counting GC vm_builtins.c 297 My runtime builtins vm_ffi.h / vm_ffi.c 611 My fork/pipe/exec FFI lifecycle cop_protocol.h / cop_protocol.c 227 My co-process wire protocol cop_main.c 212 My nano_cop binary vmd_protocol.h / vmd_protocol.c 150 My daemon wire protocol vmd_client.c 275 My daemon client connector vmd_server.c 430 My daemon server handler vmd_main.c 52 My nano_vmd binary main.c 214 My nano_vm binary

docs.title: NanoVirt (src/nanovirt/)
docs.text:
File Lines Purpose codegen.h / codegen.c 3,083 My AST to bytecode compiler wrapper_gen.h / wrapper_gen.c 574 My native executable generator main.c 331 My nano_virt binary

Total: ~11,000 lines of C

docs.title: Tests
docs.text: Test File Tests Coverage tests/nanoisa/test_nanoisa.c 470 I test ISA encoding, NVM format, assembler, disassembler tests/nanovm/test_vm.c 150 I test all VM operations, GC, cross-module calls, daemon, co-process tests/nanovm/test_codegen.c 62 I test bytecode generation from AST

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Division by zero produces 0 - I do this to match my Coq formal semantics (total division).
  • ADD is polymorphic - I handle both integer addition and string concatenation with this opcode.
  • Relative jump offsets - I use signed i32 offsets, relative to the start of the instruction.
  • String pool deduplication - I deduplicate strings at compile time to save .nvm file size.
  • Per-frame module tracking - Each call frame I create records its module for cross-module resolution.
  • Opaque proxy values - I represent FFI objects as integer IDs. I keep the actual handles in my co-process address space.

docs.title: My OS Standard Library
docs.text: I provide an OS standard library modeled after Python's os module. It allows me to interact with the underlying system in a predictable way.

docs.title: My Design Principles
docs.text:
1. POSIX-compatible. I work on Unix-like systems, including Linux, macOS, and BSD.
  2. Error handling. My functions return error codes where 0 indicates success and non-zero indicates an error.
  3. Type-safe. I use my own type system for all operations.
  4. No arrays yet. I return lists as newline-separated strings for now.

docs.title: file_read
docs.text:
I read the entire contents of a file and return it as a string.

fn file_read(path: string) -> string

Returns: File contents as string, or empty string on error. Example: let content: string = (file_read "data.txt")

docs.title: file_write
docs.text:
I write a string to a file. This overwrites any existing content.

fn file_write(path: string, content: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: assert (== (file_write "output.txt" "Hello") 0)

docs.title: file_append
docs.text:
I append a string to the end of a file.

fn file_append(path: string, content: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: (file_append "log.txt" "New entry\n")

docs.title: file_remove
docs.text:
I delete a file from the system.

fn file_remove(path: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: (file_remove "temp.txt")

docs.title: file_rename
docs.text:
I rename or move a file.

fn file_rename(old_path: string, new_path: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: (file_rename "old.txt" "new.txt")

docs.title: file_exists
docs.text:
I check if a file exists at the given path.

fn file_exists(path: string) -> bool

Returns: true if the file exists, false otherwise. Example: if (file_exists "config.txt") { ... }

docs.title: file_size
docs.text:
I retrieve the size of a file in bytes.

fn file_size(path: string) -> int

Returns: File size in bytes, or -1 on error. Example: let size: int = (file_size "data.bin")

docs.title: dir_create
docs.text:
I create a new directory.

fn dir_create(path: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: (dir_create "output")

docs.title: dir_remove
docs.text:
I remove an empty directory.

fn dir_remove(path: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: (dir_remove "temp")

docs.title: dir_list
docs.text:
I list the contents of a directory as a newline-separated string.

fn dir_list(path: string) -> string

Returns: Newline-separated list of filenames, or empty string on error. Example: let files: string = (dir_list ".")

docs.title: dir_exists
docs.text:
I check if a directory exists at the given path.

fn dir_exists(path: string) -> bool

Returns: true if the directory exists, false otherwise. Example: if (dir_exists "data") { ... }

docs.title: getcwd
docs.text:
I return my current working directory.

fn getcwd() -> string

Returns: Current directory path. Example: let cwd: string = (getcwd)

docs.title: chdir
docs.text:
I change my current working directory.

fn chdir(path: string) -> int

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error. Example: (chdir "/tmp")

docs.title: path_isfile
docs.text:
I check if a path refers to a regular file.

fn path_isfile(path: string) -> bool

Returns: true if the path is a regular file, false otherwise. Example: if (path_isfile "data.txt") { ... }

docs.title: path_isdir
docs.text:
I check if a path refers to a directory.

fn path_isdir(path: string) -> bool

Returns: true if the path is a directory, false otherwise. Example: if (path_isdir "src") { ... }

docs.title: path_join
docs.text:
I join two path components using the appropriate separator for the system.

fn path_join(a: string, b: string) -> string

Returns: Joined path string. Example: let full: string = (path_join "/home" "user") Result: "/home/user"

docs.title: path_basename
docs.text:
I extract the base name from a path.

fn path_basename(path: string) -> string

Returns: The last component of the path. Example: (path_basename "/home/user/file.txt") → "file.txt"

docs.title: path_dirname
docs.text:
I extract the directory name from a path.

fn path_dirname(path: string) -> string

Returns: The directory portion of the path. Example: (path_dirname "/home/user/file.txt") → "/home/user"

docs.title: system
docs.text:
I execute a shell command on the underlying system.

fn system(command: string) -> int

Returns: The exit code of the command. Example: let result: int = (system "ls -l")

docs.title: exit
docs.text:
I terminate the program with a specific status code.

fn exit(code: int) -> void

Returns: I never return from this call. Example: (exit 1)

docs.title: getenv
docs.text:
I retrieve the value of an environment variable.

fn getenv(name: string) -> string

Returns: The value of the variable, or an empty string if it is not set. Example: let home: string = (getenv "HOME")

docs.title: My Error Handling
docs.text:
I do not have exceptions. I use return codes instead.

  • 0: Success.
  • -1: Error. You can check errno in C for details.

For functions that return strings, I return an empty string to indicate an error. For functions that return a boolean, false can mean "not found" or indicate an error depending on the function.

docs.title: Example: File Processing Program
docs.text:
fn process_file() -> int {
    # Check if file exists
    if (not (file_exists "input.txt")) {
        print "Error: input.txt not found"
        return 1
    }

    # Read file
    let content: string = (file_read "input.txt")

    # Get file size
    let size: int = (file_size "input.txt")
    print "File size: "
    print size
    print " bytes"

    # Write to output
    let result: int = (file_write "output.txt" content)
    if (!= result 0) {
        print "Error writing file"
        return 1
    }

    return 0
}

shadow process_file {
    # Create test file
    assert (== (file_write "test_input.txt" "test data") 0)
    assert (== (process_file) 0)
    assert (file_exists "test_output.txt")
    # Cleanup
    (file_remove "test_input.txt")
    (file_remove "test_output.txt")
}

docs.title: My Implementation Notes
docs.text:
• I implement all these functions as built-ins, not user-defined functions.
  • My C transpiler generates calls to POSIX functions like open, read, write, and stat.
  • My interpreter calls C standard library functions directly.
  • I handle memory management for strings within my runtime.
  • I require a POSIX-compatible operating system.

docs.title: My Future Enhancements
docs.text:
When I add arrays: - dir_list will return array<string>. - file_readlines will return array<string>. - path_split will return array<string>.

When I add error types: - My functions will return Result<T, Error>. - I will provide better error messages and handling.

docs.title: Passive Parallelism in Nanolang: Language Design Extensions
docs.text: Status: Design Proposal Author: Rocky (do-host1) Date: 2026-03-26

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
The goal is not to give programmers more ways to ask for parallelism. Fork/join, async/await, spawn, go — these are all mechanisms that require the programmer to decompose their problem and manage concurrency explicitly. The cognitive overhead is high, and the failure modes (races, deadlocks, missed synchronization) are severe.

The goal is to make parallelism the path of least resistance — to design language constructs such that the natural way to write code is also the automatically-parallel way to execute it. The programmer expresses what they want; the runtime figures out how to compute it concurrently.

Nanolang is already close. This document identifies the specific language-level changes that would get it the rest of the way.

docs.title: Foundation: What We Already Have
docs.text:
Nanolang's existing design provides several building blocks:
