docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Introduction
  2. Lexical Structure
  3. Types
  4. 3.1 Built-in Types
  5. 3.2 Type Annotations
  6. 3.3 Type Checking
  7. 3.4 Composite Types
    • 3.4.1 Structs
    • 3.4.2 Enums
    • 3.4.3 Union Types
    • 3.4.4 Generic Types
    • 3.4.5 First-Class Function Types
    • 3.4.6 HashMap
    • Expressions
    • Statements
    • Functions
    • Shadow Tests
    • Semantics
    • Compilation Model
    • Example Programs
    • Design Rationale
    • Future Extensions

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
I am a minimal, statically-typed programming language. I prioritize clarity and am designed for machines to write and humans to read. My design rests on these principles:

  • Unambiguity: I provide one clear syntax for every semantic concept.
  • Explicitness: I do not use implicit conversions or hide my behavior.
  • Testability: I require shadow tests for every function I compile.
  • Simplicity: I maintain a minimal feature set with clear semantics.

docs.title: 2.1 Comments
docs.text:
# Single-line comment
/* Multi-line comment */

docs.title: 2.2 Identifiers
docs.text:
My identifiers must start with a letter or underscore. They can be followed by letters, digits, or underscores.

identifier = (letter | "_") { letter | digit | "_" }

Examples: x, my_var, count2, _internal

docs.title: 2.3 Keywords
docs.text:
These are my reserved keywords. You cannot use them as identifiers.

fn       let      mut      set      if       else
while    for      in       return   assert   shadow
extern   int      float    bool     string   void     
true     false    print    and      or       not
array    struct   enum     union    match

docs.title: 2.4 Literals
docs.text:
Integer Literals: A sequence of digits. You may use a leading - for negative values. 

42
-17
0

Float Literals: Digits separated by a decimal point. 

3.14
-0.5
2.0

String Literals: UTF-8 text enclosed in double quotes. 

"Hello, World!"
"nanolang"
""

Boolean Literals: 

true
false

docs.title: 2.5 Operators and Delimiters
docs.text:
(  )  {  }  ,  :  =  ->
+  -  *  /  %  ==  !=  <  <=  >  >=
and  or  not

I support both prefix notation ((+ a b)) and infix notation (a + b) for my operators. I describe this in detail in Section 4.3.

docs.title: 2.6 Whitespace
docs.text: I use whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) to separate tokens. Beyond that, I do not find it significant.

docs.title: 3.1 Built-in Types
docs.text: Type Description Example Values int 64-bit signed integer 42, -17, 0 float 64-bit floating point 3.14, -0.5 bool Boolean value true, false string UTF-8 encoded text "hello" void Absence of value (return only) N/A

docs.title: 3.2 Type Annotations
docs.text:
I require explicit type annotations for all variables and function parameters.

let x: int = 42
let name: string = "Alice"

fn add(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (+ a b)
}

docs.title: 3.3 Type Checking
docs.text:
I am statically typed. I catch all type errors at compile time.

let x: int = 42
let y: string = "hello"
# let z: int = y        # ERROR: Type mismatch
# let w: int = (+ x y)  # ERROR: Cannot add int and string

docs.title: 3.4.1 Structs
docs.text:
I use structs to group related data.

struct Point {
    x: int,
    y: int
}

let p: Point = Point { x: 10, y: 20 }
let x_coord: int = p.x

docs.title: 3.4.2 Enums
docs.text:
I define enums as a fixed set of named constants. I treat these constants as integers.

enum Status {
    Pending = 0,
    Active = 1,
    Complete = 2
}

let s: int = Status.Active

docs.title: 3.4.3 Union Types
docs.text:
My union types represent a value that can be one of several variants. These are tagged unions.

union Result {
    Ok { value: int },
    Error { code: int, message: string }
}

fn divide(a: int, b: int) -> Result {
    if (== b 0) {
        return Result.Error { code: 1, message: "Division by zero" }
    } else {
        return Result.Ok { value: (/ a b) }
    }
}

Union Construction:

# Empty variant
let status: Status = Status.Ok {}

# Variant with fields
let result: Result = Result.Error { code: 1, message: "Failed" }

Pattern Matching:

I use the match expression to destructure unions.

match result {
    Ok(r) => (println "Success"),
    Error(e) => (println "Error")
}

docs.title: 3.4.4 Generic Types
docs.text:
I use generic types to allow parameterization. I implement these using monomorphization. I specialize generic types at compile time for each concrete type you use.

Built-in Generic: List 

# Create typed lists
let numbers: List<int> = (List_int_new)
(List_int_push numbers 1)
(List_int_push numbers 2)
(List_int_push numbers 3)

let names: List<string> = (List_string_new)
(List_string_push names "Alice")
(List_string_push names "Bob")

# Lists with user-defined types
struct Point { x: int, y: int }
let points: List<Point> = (List_Point_new)

Generic Instantiation:

When you use a generic type like List<int>, I generate specialized functions for that type: - List_int_new() - List_int_push(list, value) - List_int_length(list) - List_int_get(list, index)

Monomorphization:

I generate a separate implementation for each concrete type you use with a generic.

let integers: List<int> = (List_int_new)     # I generate List_int functions
let strings: List<string> = (List_string_new) # I generate List_string functions

I generate specialized C code for each instantiation to avoid runtime overhead.

docs.title: 3.4.5 First-Class Function Types
docs.text:
I treat functions as first-class values. You can pass them as parameters, return them from other functions, or assign them to variables.

Function Type Syntax:

fn(param_type1, param_type2) -> return_type

Function Variables:

fn double(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x 2)
}

shadow double {
    assert (== (double 5) 10)
}

# Assign function to variable
let f: fn(int) -> int = double

# Call through variable
let result: int = (f 7)  # result = 14

Functions as Parameters:

fn apply_twice(op: fn(int) -> int, x: int) -> int {
    return (op (op x))
}

shadow apply_twice {
    assert (== (apply_twice double 5) 20)
}

Functions as Return Values:

fn get_operation(choice: int) -> fn(int) -> int {
    if (== choice 0) {
        return double
    } else {
        return triple
    }
}

shadow get_operation {
    let op: fn(int) -> int = (get_operation 0)
    assert (== (op 5) 10)
}

My function types do not expose underlying C function pointers. I treat them as opaque values.

docs.title: 4.1 Literals
docs.text:
I evaluate literals to their corresponding values.

42          # int
3.14        # float
"hello"     # string
true        # bool

docs.title: 4.2 Variables
docs.text:
I evaluate identifiers to the value of the named variable.

let x: int = 42
let y: int = x  # y = 42

docs.title: 4.3 Operations (Prefix and Infix)
docs.text:
I support both prefix notation and infix notation for my binary operators.

My prefix notation uses parentheses.

(+ 2 3)              # 5
(* (+ 2 3) 4)        # 20
(== x 5)             # Comparison
(and (> x 0) (< x 10))  # Logical AND

My infix notation uses standard operator placement.

2 + 3                # 5
(2 + 3) * 4          # 20
x == 5               # Comparison
x > 0 and x < 10    # Logical AND

My infix operators are: +, -, *, /, %, ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=, and, or.

Precedence: I assign equal precedence to all my infix operators. I evaluate them strictly from left to right. I do not use PEMDAS. You must use parentheses if you want to control grouping.

a * (b + c)          # I add before I multiply
a + b * c            # I evaluate this as (a + b) * c

My unary operators not and - work without parentheses.

not flag             # Logical negation
-x                   # Numeric negation

You can mix these notations. I maintain prefix notation for compatibility.

docs.title: 4.4 Arithmetic Operations
docs.text: Operator Description Type Signature + Addition (int, int) -> int or (float, float) -> float - Subtraction (int, int) -> int or (float, float) -> float * Multiplication (int, int) -> int or (float, float) -> float / Division (int, int) -> int or (float, float) -> float % Modulo (int, int) -> int

docs.title: 4.5 Comparison Operations
docs.text:
My comparison operations all return a bool.

Operator Description Type Signature == Equal (T, T) -> bool != Not equal (T, T) -> bool < Less than (int, int) -> bool or (float, float) -> bool <= Less or equal (int, int) -> bool or (float, float) -> bool > Greater than (int, int) -> bool or (float, float) -> bool >= Greater or equal (int, int) -> bool or (float, float) -> bool

docs.title: 4.6 Logical Operations
docs.text: Operator Description Type Signature and Logical AND (bool, bool) -> bool or Logical OR (bool, bool) -> bool not Logical NOT (bool) -> bool

docs.title: 4.7 Function Calls
docs.text:
I always use prefix notation for function calls.

(add 2 3)
(multiply (add 1 2) 4)
(is_prime 17)
(println "hello")

docs.title: 4.8 If Expressions
docs.text:
I treat if as an expression that returns a value.

let x: int = if (> a 0) {
    42
} else {
    -1
}

I require both branches to return the same type. I require an else branch.

I support else if chaining for multiple conditions.

if x > 10 {
    (println "big")
} else if x > 5 {
    (println "medium")
} else {
    (println "small")
}

docs.title: 4.9 Evaluation Order
docs.text:
I evaluate expressions from left to right.

(+ (f x) (g y))  # I evaluate f(x) before g(y)
(f x) + (g y)    # I evaluate f(x) before g(y)

docs.title: 5.1 Variable Declaration
docs.text:
I use let to declare variables.

let x: int = 42
let mut counter: int = 0

I make variables immutable by default. You must use mut to declare a mutable variable.

docs.title: 5.2 Assignment
docs.text:
I only allow you to reassign mutable variables. I use set for this.

let mut x: int = 0
set x (+ x 1)
# let y: int = 0
# set y 1  # ERROR: y is not mutable

docs.title: 5.3 While Loop
docs.text:
while condition {
    # body
}

I require the condition to be a bool expression. I execute the loop while the condition is true.

let mut i: int = 0
while (< i 10) {
    print i
    set i (+ i 1)
}

docs.title: 5.4 For Loop
docs.text:
for identifier in expression {
    # body
}

I provide the for loop as a way to iterate over a range.

for i in (range 0 10) {
    print i
}

# This is equivalent to:
let mut i: int = 0
while (< i 10) {
    print i
    set i (+ i 1)
}

docs.title: 5.5 Return Statement
docs.text:
return expression

I use this to return a value from a function. I check that the expression type matches the return type I defined for the function.

docs.title: 5.6 Expression Statement
docs.text:
I allow any expression to be used as a statement.

print "hello"
(add 2 3)  # I discard the result

docs.title: 6.1 Function Definition
docs.text:
fn name(param1: type1, param2: type2) -> return_type {
    # body
}

I require that every function: 1. Defines explicit parameter types. 2. Defines an explicit return type. 3. Returns a value if the return type is not void. 4. Includes a shadow test.

docs.title: 6.2 Parameters
docs.text:
I pass parameters by value. I make them immutable within the function.

fn increment(x: int) -> int {
    # set x (+ x 1)  # ERROR: Parameters are immutable
    return (+ x 1)
}

docs.title: 6.3 Return Type
docs.text:
I require a return type for every function. - If the function is not void, I ensure every code path returns a value. - If the function is void, you can use return without a value or omit it.

fn get_sign(x: int) -> int {
    if (> x 0) {
        return 1
    } else {
        if (< x 0) {
            return -1
        } else {
            return 0
        }
    }
}

fn greet() -> void {
    print "Hello"
}

docs.title: 6.4 External Functions (FFI)
docs.text:
I allow you to declare external functions to call the C standard library.

extern fn function_name(param: type) -> return_type

These declarations have no body and do not require a shadow test. I call them using their C names. I require these functions to be safe.

# Declare external C functions
extern fn sqrt(x: float) -> float
extern fn pow(x: float, y: float) -> float
extern fn isdigit(c: int) -> int
extern fn strlen(s: string) -> int

# Use them in my code
fn hypotenuse(a: float, b: float) -> float {
    let a_sq: float = (pow a 2.0)
    let b_sq: float = (pow b 2.0)
    return (sqrt (+ a_sq b_sq))
}

shadow hypotenuse {
    assert (== (hypotenuse 3.0 4.0) 5.0)
}

I expect you to only expose C functions that are safe. They should use explicit lengths and avoid pointer arithmetic.

docs.title: 7.1 Purpose
docs.text: I use shadow tests to ensure honesty. - I run them during compilation. - I stop compilation if a test fails. - I remove them from production builds. - I use them to document my behavior.

docs.title: 7.2 Syntax
docs.text:
shadow function_name {
    # test body with assertions
}

I require exactly one shadow test block for every function you define. You must place it after the function definition.

docs.title: 7.3 Assertions
docs.text:
I use assert within shadow tests to verify my behavior.

fn add(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (+ a b)
}

shadow add {
    assert (== (add 2 3) 5)
    assert (== (add 0 0) 0)
    assert (== (add -5 3) -2)
}

docs.title: 7.4 Assertion Semantics
docs.text: I evaluate the boolean expression given to assert. - If it is true, I continue. - If it is false, I stop compilation and report the error.

docs.title: 7.5 Coverage Requirements
docs.text: I expect shadow tests to cover normal cases, edge cases, and boundary conditions.

docs.title: 7.6 Execution Order
docs.text: I run each shadow test immediately after I finish defining the function it tests.

docs.title: 8.1 Static Scoping
docs.text:
I use static scoping. I resolve variables when I compile your code.

let x: int = 1

fn f() -> int {
    return x  # This is the global x
}

fn g() -> int {
    let x: int = 2
    return (f)  # I return 1
}

shadow f {
    assert (== (f) 1)
}

shadow g {
    assert (== (g) 1)
}

docs.title: 8.2 Variable Shadowing
docs.text:
I allow inner scopes to shadow variables from outer scopes.

let x: int = 1
{
    let x: int = 2  # I shadow the outer x
    print x         # I print 2
}
print x            # I print 1

docs.title: 8.3 Type Equivalence
docs.text: I consider types equivalent only if they share the same name. I do not use structural typing.

docs.title: 8.4 No Implicit Conversions
docs.text:
I require all type conversions to be explicit.

let x: int = 42
# let y: float = x  # ERROR: I do not convert implicitly

docs.title: 8.5 Short-Circuit Evaluation
docs.text:
I use short-circuit evaluation for and and or.

(and false (expensive_computation))  # I do not call expensive_computation
(or true (expensive_computation))    # I do not call expensive_computation

docs.title: 9.1 Phases
docs.text:
1. Lexing: I turn your source text into tokens.
  2. Parsing: I turn those tokens into an AST.
  3. Type Checking: I verify your types, tests, and return paths.
  4. Shadow Test Execution: I run your tests.
  5. Transpilation: I turn my AST into C code.
  6. C Compilation: I use a C compiler to produce a binary.

docs.title: 9.2 Shadow Test Compilation
docs.text: I extract tests during parsing and check them for correctness. I execute them while I compile. I do not include them in my final output.

docs.title: 9.3 C Transpilation
docs.text:
I produce clean C code.

fn add(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (+ a b)
}

I turn that into:

int64_t add(int64_t a, int64_t b) {
    return a + b;
}

docs.title: 9.4 Entry Point
docs.text:
I require you to define a main function.

fn main() -> int {
    return 0
}

shadow main {
    assert (== (main) 0)
}

docs.title: 9.5 Built-in Functions
docs.text:
I provide built-in functions through my runtime. I currently offer 72 functions across several categories.

Core I/O: - print, println - assert

Math Operations: - abs, min, max, sqrt, pow, floor, ceil, round, sin, cos, tan

String Operations: - str_length, str_concat, str_substring, str_contains, str_equals - char_at, string_from_char, is_digit, is_alpha, is_alnum, is_whitespace, is_upper, is_lower - int_to_string, string_to_int, digit_value, char_to_lower, char_to_upper

Array Operations: - at, array_length, array_new, array_set

List Operations: - list_int_* operations for dynamic lists.

OS Operations: - Functions for files, directories, paths, and system commands.

Iteration: - range

I have documented these in STDLIB.md.

docs.title: 10.1 Fibonacci
docs.text:
fn fib(n: int) -> int {
    if (<= n 1) {
        return n
    } else {
        return (+ (fib (- n 1)) (fib (- n 2)))
    }
}

shadow fib {
    assert (== (fib 0) 0)
    assert (== (fib 1) 1)
    assert (== (fib 2) 1)
    assert (== (fib 5) 5)
    assert (== (fib 10) 55)
}

fn main() -> int {
    print (fib 10)
    return 0
}

shadow main {
    assert (== (main) 0)
}

docs.title: 10.2 Prime Number Checker
docs.text:
fn is_prime(n: int) -> bool {
    if (< n 2) {
        return false
    }
    let mut i: int = 2
    while (< i n) {
        if (== (% n i) 0) {
            return false
        }
        set i (+ i 1)
    }
    return true
}

shadow is_prime {
    assert (== (is_prime 1) false)
    assert (== (is_prime 2) true)
    assert (== (is_prime 3) true)
    assert (== (is_prime 4) false)
    assert (== (is_prime 17) true)
    assert (== (is_prime 100) false)
}

fn main() -> int {
    for n in (range 1 20) {
        if (is_prime n) {
            print n
        }
    }
    return 0
}

shadow main {
    assert (== (main) 0)
}

docs.title: 11.1 Why Both Prefix and Infix Notation?
docs.text:
I support both notations to balance precision and familiarity. My prefix notation makes nesting explicit and removes ambiguity. This is helpful for machines. My infix notation is familiar to humans.

To avoid traditional precedence issues, I give all my infix operators equal precedence. I evaluate them from left to right. I require you to use parentheses if you want a different order.

I only use prefix notation for my function calls. This keeps them distinct from my operators.

docs.title: 11.2 Why Mandatory Shadow Tests?
docs.text: I require tests because untested code is a claim without proof. My tests also serve as documentation and give you confidence in my correctness.

docs.title: 11.3 Why Static Typing?
docs.text: I use static typing to catch errors before your code ever runs. It makes my semantics clearer and helps machines generate correct code.

docs.title: 11.4 Why C Transpilation?
docs.text: I transpile to C because it is portable and efficient. It allows me to use existing tools and will eventually allow me to compile myself.

docs.title: 12. Future Extensions
docs.text:
What I have implemented in v0.1: - Arrays (static and dynamic) - Structs - Enums - Unions - Generics (List and HashMap) - First-class functions - Pattern matching - My standard library - My C transpiler

What I am developing: - Tuple types - My self-hosted compiler

My plans for the future: - A module system - More generic types - Async primitives - Memory management hints - A package manager

I will ensure that all my extensions follow my core principles. I will remain minimal and unambiguous. I will always require tests.

docs.title: My Standard Library Reference
docs.text: I provide these built-in functions. They are my core capabilities, available to you without external modules.

docs.title: print(value: any) -> void
docs.text:
I print a value without a trailing newline.

(print "Hello")
(print 42)
(print 3.14)

docs.title: println(value: any) -> void
docs.text:
I print a value with a trailing newline. I am polymorphic — I work with int, float, string, and bool.

(println "Hello, World!")
(println 42)
(println true)

docs.title: range(start: int, end: int) -> iterator
docs.text:
I provide this special function for use only in for loops. I create an iterator from start (inclusive) to end (exclusive).

for i in (range 0 10) {
    (println i)  # Prints 0, 1, 2, ..., 9
}
for i in (range 5 8) {
    (println i)  # Prints 5, 6, 7
}

I only allow range to be used in for-loop contexts.

docs.title: abs(x: int) -> int
docs.text:
I return the absolute value of an integer.

(abs -5)    # Returns 5
(abs 5)     # Returns 5
(abs 0)     # Returns 0

docs.title: min(a: int, b: int) -> int
docs.text:
I return the minimum of two integers.

(min 5 10)   # Returns 5
(min -3 0)   # Returns -3
(min 7 7)    # Returns 7

docs.title: max(a: int, b: int) -> int
docs.text:
I return the maximum of two integers.

(max 5 10)   # Returns 10
(max -3 0)   # Returns 0
(max 7 7)    # Returns 7

docs.title: sqrt(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the square root of x.

(sqrt 16.0)   # Returns 4.0
(sqrt 2.0)    # Returns 1.41421...
(sqrt 9.0)    # Returns 3.0

docs.title: pow(base: float, exponent: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return base raised to the power of exponent.

(pow 2.0 3.0)    # Returns 8.0
(pow 5.0 2.0)    # Returns 25.0
(pow 2.0 -1.0)   # Returns 0.5

docs.title: floor(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the largest integer ≤ x as a float.

(floor 3.7)    # Returns 3.0
(floor 3.2)    # Returns 3.0
(floor -2.3)   # Returns -3.0

docs.title: ceil(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the smallest integer ≥ x as a float.

(ceil 3.2)    # Returns 4.0
(ceil 3.7)    # Returns 4.0
(ceil -2.7)   # Returns -2.0

docs.title: round(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I round to the nearest integer as a float.

(round 3.4)   # Returns 3.0
(round 3.6)   # Returns 4.0
(round 3.5)   # Returns 4.0

docs.title: sin(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the sine of x in radians.

(sin 0.0)      # Returns 0.0
(sin 1.5708)   # Returns ≈1.0 (π/2)
(sin 3.14159)  # Returns ≈0.0 (π)

docs.title: cos(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the cosine of x in radians.

(cos 0.0)      # Returns 1.0
(cos 3.14159)  # Returns ≈-1.0 (π)
(cos 1.5708)   # Returns ≈0.0 (π/2)

docs.title: tan(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the tangent of x in radians.

(tan 0.0)     # Returns 0.0
(tan 0.7854)  # Returns ≈1.0 (π/4)
(tan 1.0)     # Returns ≈1.5574

docs.title: atan2(y: float, x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the angle in radians between the positive x-axis and the point (x, y). I handle the quadrant correctly, unlike atan.

(atan2 1.0 1.0)    # Returns ≈0.7854 (π/4, first quadrant)
(atan2 1.0 -1.0)   # Returns ≈2.3562 (3π/4, second quadrant)
(atan2 0.0 1.0)    # Returns 0.0

docs.title: asin(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the arcsine of x (inverse sine) in radians. My domain is -1.0 to 1.0 and I return values in -π/2 to π/2.

(asin 1.0)    # Returns ≈1.5708 (π/2)
(asin 0.5)    # Returns ≈0.5236 (π/6)
(asin 0.0)    # Returns 0.0

docs.title: acos(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the arccosine of x (inverse cosine) in radians. My domain is -1.0 to 1.0 and I return values in 0 to π.

(acos 1.0)    # Returns 0.0
(acos 0.0)    # Returns ≈1.5708 (π/2)
(acos -1.0)   # Returns ≈3.14159 (π)

docs.title: atan(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the arctangent of x (inverse tangent) in radians. I return values in -π/2 to π/2.

(atan 1.0)    # Returns ≈0.7854 (π/4)
(atan 0.0)    # Returns 0.0
(atan -1.0)   # Returns ≈-0.7854 (-π/4)

docs.title: log(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the natural logarithm (base e) of x.

(log 1.0)    # Returns 0.0
(log 2.718)  # Returns ≈1.0
(log 10.0)   # Returns ≈2.303

docs.title: log2(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the base-2 logarithm of x.

(log2 1.0)   # Returns 0.0
(log2 2.0)   # Returns 1.0
(log2 8.0)   # Returns 3.0

docs.title: log10(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the base-10 logarithm of x.

(log10 1.0)    # Returns 0.0
(log10 10.0)   # Returns 1.0
(log10 100.0)  # Returns 2.0

docs.title: exp(x: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return e raised to the power of x.

(exp 0.0)   # Returns 1.0
(exp 1.0)   # Returns ≈2.71828
(exp 2.0)   # Returns ≈7.389

docs.title: fmod(x: float, y: float) -> float
docs.text:
I return the floating-point remainder of x divided by y.

(fmod 5.5 2.0)    # Returns 1.5
(fmod 10.0 3.0)   # Returns 1.0
(fmod -5.5 2.0)   # Returns -1.5

docs.title: cast_int(value: any) -> int
docs.text:
I cast any value to an integer. I truncate floats and parse strings.

(cast_int 3.14)   # Returns 3
(cast_int "42")   # Returns 42
(cast_int true)   # Returns 1

docs.title: cast_float(value: any) -> float
docs.text:
I cast any value to a float. I parse strings and convert integers.

(cast_float 42)      # Returns 42.0
(cast_float "3.14")  # Returns 3.14
(cast_float false)   # Returns 0.0

docs.title: cast_bool(value: any) -> bool
docs.text:
I cast any value to a boolean. I treat 0, empty string, and null as false; everything else becomes true.

(cast_bool 1)       # Returns true
(cast_bool 0)       # Returns false
(cast_bool "hello") # Returns true
(cast_bool "")      # Returns false

docs.title: cast_string(value: any) -> string
docs.text:
I cast any value to its string representation.

(cast_string 42)    # Returns "42"
(cast_string 3.14)  # Returns "3.14"
(cast_string true)  # Returns "true"

docs.title: to_string(value: any) -> string
docs.text:
I convert any value to its string representation. I am an alias for cast_string.

(to_string 99)      # Returns "99"
(to_string false)   # Returns "false"
(to_string 1.5)     # Returns "1.5"

docs.title: int_to_string(n: int) -> string
docs.text:
I convert an integer to its string representation.

(int_to_string 42)    # Returns "42"
(int_to_string 0)     # Returns "0"
(int_to_string -100)  # Returns "-100"

docs.title: float_to_string(f: float) -> string
docs.text:
I convert a float to its string representation.

(float_to_string 3.14)   # Returns "3.14"
(float_to_string 0.0)    # Returns "0.0"
(float_to_string -1.5)   # Returns "-1.5"

docs.title: bool_to_string(b: bool) -> string
docs.text:
I convert a boolean to its string representation.

(bool_to_string true)   # Returns "true"
(bool_to_string false)  # Returns "false"

docs.title: string_to_int(s: string) -> int
docs.text:
I parse a string to an integer. I return 0 if the string cannot be parsed.

(string_to_int "42")     # Returns 42
(string_to_int "-100")   # Returns -100
(string_to_int "abc")    # Returns 0

docs.title: string_to_float(s: string) -> float
docs.text:
I parse a string to a float. I return 0.0 if the string cannot be parsed.

(string_to_float "3.14")  # Returns 3.14
(string_to_float "1e-3")  # Returns 0.001
(string_to_float "bad")   # Returns 0.0

docs.title: null_opaque() -> opaque
docs.text:
I return a null opaque handle. I am useful as a sentinel value when working with foreign function interfaces.

let handle: opaque = (null_opaque)

docs.title: str_length(s: string) -> int
docs.text:
I return the length of a string in bytes.

(str_length "Hello")   # Returns 5
(str_length "")        # Returns 0
(str_length "abc")     # Returns 3

docs.title: str_concat(s1: string, s2: string) -> string
docs.text:
I concatenate two strings and return a new string.

(str_concat "Hello" " World")   # Returns "Hello World"
(str_concat "foo" "bar")        # Returns "foobar"
(str_concat "" "test")          # Returns "test"

docs.title: str_substring(s: string, start: int, length: int) -> string
docs.text:
I extract a substring starting at start with the given length. The index is 0-based. I return until the end of the string if start + length exceeds the string length. I return an empty string if start is out of bounds.

(str_substring "Hello, World!" 0 5)   # Returns "Hello"
(str_substring "Hello, World!" 7 5)   # Returns "World"
(str_substring "Hello" 2 100)         # Returns "llo"

docs.title: str_contains(s: string, substr: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if string s contains substring substr.

(str_contains "The quick brown fox" "quick")   # Returns true
(str_contains "The quick brown fox" "slow")    # Returns false
(str_contains "hello" "")                      # Returns true

docs.title: str_equals(s1: string, s2: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if both strings are exactly equal.

(str_equals "Hello" "Hello")   # Returns true
(str_equals "Hello" "World")   # Returns false
(str_equals "" "")             # Returns true

docs.title: char_at(s: string, index: int) -> int
docs.text:
I return the ASCII value of the character at the specified 0-based index. I terminate with an error if the index is out of bounds.

(char_at "Hello" 0)   # Returns 72 ('H')
(char_at "Hello" 1)   # Returns 101 ('e')
(char_at "Hello" 4)   # Returns 111 ('o')

docs.title: string_from_char(c: int) -> string
docs.text:
I create a single-character string from an ASCII value.

(string_from_char 65)   # Returns "A"
(string_from_char 90)   # Returns "Z"
(string_from_char 48)   # Returns "0"

docs.title: is_digit(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code represents a decimal digit ('0'–'9').

(is_digit 48)   # Returns true  ('0')
(is_digit 53)   # Returns true  ('5')
(is_digit 65)   # Returns false ('A')

docs.title: is_alpha(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code represents a letter (a–z, A–Z).

(is_alpha 65)    # Returns true  ('A')
(is_alpha 97)    # Returns true  ('a')
(is_alpha 48)    # Returns false ('0')

docs.title: is_alnum(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code represents an alphanumeric character (digit or letter).

(is_alnum 48)   # Returns true  ('0')
(is_alnum 65)   # Returns true  ('A')
(is_alnum 32)   # Returns false (' ')

docs.title: is_space(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code is a space character (ASCII 32).

(is_space 32)   # Returns true  (' ')
(is_space 65)   # Returns false ('A')
(is_space 9)    # Returns false ('\t')

docs.title: is_whitespace(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code represents any whitespace: space, tab, newline, or carriage return.

(is_whitespace 32)   # Returns true  (' ')
(is_whitespace 9)    # Returns true  ('\t')
(is_whitespace 10)   # Returns true  ('\n')
(is_whitespace 65)   # Returns false ('A')

docs.title: is_upper(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code represents an uppercase letter (A–Z).

(is_upper 65)   # Returns true  ('A')
(is_upper 90)   # Returns true  ('Z')
(is_upper 97)   # Returns false ('a')

docs.title: is_lower(c: int) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the character code represents a lowercase letter (a–z).

(is_lower 97)    # Returns true  ('a')
(is_lower 122)   # Returns true  ('z')
(is_lower 65)    # Returns false ('A')

docs.title: digit_value(c: int) -> int
docs.text:
I convert a digit character code to its numeric value. I return -1 if it is not a digit.

(digit_value 48)   # Returns 0  ('0' -> 0)
(digit_value 53)   # Returns 5  ('5' -> 5)
(digit_value 57)   # Returns 9  ('9' -> 9)
(digit_value 65)   # Returns -1 ('A' is not a digit)

docs.title: char_to_lower(c: int) -> int
docs.text:
I convert an uppercase letter code to lowercase. I leave non-letters unchanged.

(char_to_lower 65)   # Returns 97  ('A' -> 'a')
(char_to_lower 90)   # Returns 122 ('Z' -> 'z')
(char_to_lower 48)   # Returns 48  ('0' -> '0', unchanged)

docs.title: char_to_upper(c: int) -> int
docs.text:
I convert a lowercase letter code to uppercase. I leave non-letters unchanged.

(char_to_upper 97)    # Returns 65  ('a' -> 'A')
(char_to_upper 122)   # Returns 90  ('z' -> 'Z')
(char_to_upper 48)    # Returns 48  ('0' -> '0', unchanged)

docs.title: at(arr: array<T>, index: int) -> T
docs.text:
I return the element at the specified 0-based index. I perform bounds-checking and terminate with an error if the index is out of bounds.

let nums: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
(at nums 0)   # Returns 1
(at nums 4)   # Returns 5

docs.title: array_get(arr: array<T>, index: int) -> T
docs.text:
I return the element at the specified 0-based index. I am an alias for at.

let nums: array<int> = [10, 20, 30]
(array_get nums 0)   # Returns 10
(array_get nums 2)   # Returns 30

docs.title: array_length(arr: array<T>) -> int
docs.text:
I return the number of elements in an array.

let nums: array<int> = [10, 20, 30]
(array_length nums)    # Returns 3
(array_length [])      # Returns 0

docs.title: array_new(size: int, default: T) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I create a new array of the specified size, filled with the default value.

let zeros: array<int> = (array_new 5 0)
# [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
let strs: array<string> = (array_new 3 "")
# ["", "", ""]

docs.title: array_set(arr: array<T>, index: int, value: T) -> void
docs.text:
I set the element at the specified 0-based index. I perform bounds-checking and terminate with an error if the index is out of bounds. I require a mutable array.

let mut nums: array<int> = [1, 2, 3]
(array_set nums 1 42)
# nums is now [1, 42, 3]

docs.title: array_push(arr: array<T>, value: T) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I append an element to the end of an array and return the updated array.

let mut numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3]
(array_push numbers 4)
# numbers is now [1, 2, 3, 4]

docs.title: array_pop(arr: array<T>) -> T
docs.text:
I remove and return the last element of the array.

let mut stack: array<int> = [1, 2, 3]
let last: int = (array_pop stack)   # Returns 3
# stack is now [1, 2]

docs.title: array_remove_at(arr: array<T>, index: int) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I remove the element at the specified index, shifting remaining elements left, and return the updated array.

let mut items: array<int> = [10, 20, 30, 40]
(array_remove_at items 1)
# items is now [10, 30, 40]

docs.title: array_slice(arr: array<T>, start: int, length: int) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I create a new array from a portion of the original, starting at start with the given length.

let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
let subset: array<int> = (array_slice numbers 1 3)
# subset is [2, 3, 4]

docs.title: array_concat(arr1: array<T>, arr2: array<T>) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I concatenate two arrays and return a new array containing all elements.

let a: array<int> = [1, 2, 3]
let b: array<int> = [4, 5, 6]
let c: array<int> = (array_concat a b)
# c is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

docs.title: array_map(arr: array<T>, f: fn(T) -> U) -> array<U>
docs.text:
I apply a function to each element of an array and return a new array of the results.

fn square(x: int) -> int { return (* x x) }

let nums: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4]
let squares: array<int> = (array_map nums square)
# squares is [1, 4, 9, 16]

docs.title: array_filter(arr: array<T>, pred: fn(T) -> bool) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I return a new array containing only the elements for which the predicate returns true.

fn is_even(n: int) -> bool { return (== (% n 2) 0) }

let nums: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
let evens: array<int> = (array_filter nums is_even)
# evens is [2, 4, 6]

docs.title: array_fold(arr: array<T>, init: U, f: fn(U, T) -> U) -> U
docs.text:
I reduce an array to a single value by applying a function cumulatively, starting with init.

fn add(acc: int, x: int) -> int { return (+ acc x) }

let nums: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4]
let sum: int = (array_fold nums 0 add)
# sum is 10

docs.title: filter(arr: array<T>, predicate: fn(T) -> bool) -> array<T>
docs.text:
I create a new array with elements that satisfy the predicate. I am equivalent to array_filter.

fn is_even(n: int) -> bool { return (== (% n 2) 0) }

let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
let evens: array<int> = (filter numbers is_even)
# evens is [2, 4, 6]

docs.title: map(arr: array<T>, f: fn(T) -> U) -> array<U>
docs.text:
I transform each element using the provided function. I am equivalent to array_map.

fn square(x: int) -> int { return (* x x) }

let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4]
let squares: array<int> = (map numbers square)
# squares is [1, 4, 9, 16]

docs.title: reduce(arr: array<T>, init: U, f: fn(U, T) -> U) -> U
docs.text:
I reduce an array to a single value. I am equivalent to array_fold.

fn add(acc: int, x: int) -> int { return (+ acc x) }

let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4]
let sum: int = (reduce numbers 0 add)
# sum is 10

docs.title: HashMap Operations (16)
docs.text: I provide HashMap as a key-value collection with O(1) average lookup.

docs.title: hashmap_new() -> hashmap
docs.text:
I create a new empty hashmap.

let hm: hashmap = (hashmap_new)

docs.title: hashmap_get(hm: hashmap, key: string) -> any
docs.text:
I return the value associated with key, or null if the key does not exist.

(hashmap_set hm "name" "Alice")
let val: string = (hashmap_get hm "name")   # Returns "Alice"

docs.title: hashmap_set(hm: hashmap, key: string, value: any) -> void
docs.text:
I insert or update the value for key.

let hm: hashmap = (hashmap_new)
(hashmap_set hm "score" 100)
(hashmap_set hm "name" "Bob")

docs.title: hashmap_has(hm: hashmap, key: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if key exists in the hashmap.

(hashmap_set hm "x" 42)
(hashmap_has hm "x")       # Returns true
(hashmap_has hm "missing")  # Returns false

docs.title: hashmap_delete(hm: hashmap, key: string) -> void
docs.text:
I remove the key-value pair for key. I do nothing if the key does not exist.

(hashmap_set hm "temp" 99)
(hashmap_delete hm "temp")
(hashmap_has hm "temp")   # Returns false

docs.title: hashmap_keys(hm: hashmap) -> array<string>
docs.text:
I return all keys in the hashmap as an array of strings.

(hashmap_set hm "a" 1)
(hashmap_set hm "b" 2)
let keys: array<string> = (hashmap_keys hm)
# keys contains ["a", "b"] (order may vary)

docs.title: hashmap_values(hm: hashmap) -> array<any>
docs.text:
I return all values in the hashmap as an array.

(hashmap_set hm "x" 10)
(hashmap_set hm "y" 20)
let vals: array<int> = (hashmap_values hm)
# vals contains [10, 20] (order may vary)

docs.title: hashmap_length(hm: hashmap) -> int
docs.text:
I return the number of key-value pairs in the hashmap.

let hm: hashmap = (hashmap_new)
(hashmap_set hm "a" 1)
(hashmap_set hm "b" 2)
(hashmap_length hm)   # Returns 2

docs.title: map_new() -> hashmap
docs.text:
I create a new empty hashmap. I am an alias for hashmap_new.

let m: hashmap = (map_new)

docs.title: map_get(hm: hashmap, key: string) -> any
docs.text:
I return the value for key. I am an alias for hashmap_get.

let score: int = (map_get hm "alice")

docs.title: map_set(hm: hashmap, key: string, value: any) -> void
docs.text:
I insert or update a key-value pair. I am an alias for hashmap_set.

(map_set hm "alice" 10)
(map_set hm "bob" 20)

docs.title: map_has(hm: hashmap, key: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I check if a key exists. I am an alias for hashmap_has.

if (map_has hm "alice") { (println "found") }

docs.title: map_delete(hm: hashmap, key: string) -> void
docs.text:
I remove a key-value pair. I am an alias for hashmap_delete.

(map_delete hm "temp")

docs.title: map_keys(hm: hashmap) -> array<string>
docs.text:
I return all keys as an array. I am an alias for hashmap_keys.

let keys: array<string> = (map_keys hm)

docs.title: map_values(hm: hashmap) -> array<any>
docs.text:
I return all values as an array. I am an alias for hashmap_values.

let vals: array<int> = (map_values hm)

docs.title: map_length(hm: hashmap) -> int
docs.text:
I return the number of entries. I am an alias for hashmap_length.

let count: int = (map_length hm)

docs.title: Result Type Operations (7)
docs.text: My Result<T, E> type represents either success (Ok) or failure (Err).

docs.title: result_is_ok(r: Result<T, E>) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the result is an Ok value.

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 2)
if (result_is_ok r) { (println "Success!") }

docs.title: result_is_err(r: Result<T, E>) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the result is an Err value.

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 0)
if (result_is_err r) { (println "Error occurred") }

docs.title: result_unwrap(r: Result<T, E>) -> T
docs.text:
I extract the Ok value. I panic if the result is Err — use result_is_ok to check first.

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 2)
let value: int = (result_unwrap r)   # Returns 5

docs.title: result_unwrap_err(r: Result<T, E>) -> E
docs.text:
I extract the Err value. I panic if the result is Ok.

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 0)
if (result_is_err r) {
    let msg: string = (result_unwrap_err r)
    (println msg)
}

docs.title: result_unwrap_or(r: Result<T, E>, default: T) -> T
docs.text:
I extract the Ok value, or return default if the result is Err.

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 0)
let value: int = (result_unwrap_or r 0)   # Returns 0 (the default)

docs.title: result_map(r: Result<T, E>, f: fn(T) -> U) -> Result<U, E>
docs.text:
I apply a function to the Ok value and return a new result. I pass Err values through unchanged.

fn double(x: int) -> int { return (* x 2) }

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 2)
let r2: Result<int, string> = (result_map r double)
# r2 is Ok(10)

docs.title: result_and_then(r: Result<T, E>, f: fn(T) -> Result<U, E>) -> Result<U, E>
docs.text:
I apply a function that itself returns a Result, and flatten the result. I pass Err values through unchanged. I use this to chain fallible operations.

fn safe_sqrt(x: int) -> Result<float, string> {
    if (< x 0) { return (Err "negative input") }
    return (Ok (sqrt (cast_float x)))
}

let r: Result<int, string> = (divide 16 1)
let r2: Result<float, string> = (result_and_then r safe_sqrt)
# r2 is Ok(4.0)

docs.title: file_read(path: string) -> string
docs.text:
I read the entire contents of a file and return them as a string. I return an empty string on error.

let content: string = (file_read "data.txt")
(println content)

docs.title: file_read_bytes(path: string) -> array<int>
docs.text:
I read file contents as an array of byte values (0–255). I recommend this for binary files.

let data: array<int> = (file_read_bytes "image.png")
let size: int = (array_length data)

docs.title: file_write(path: string, content: string) -> int
docs.text:
I write string content to a file, overwriting it if it already exists. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (file_write "output.txt" "Hello, World!")
if (== status 0) { (println "Write successful") }

docs.title: file_append(path: string, content: string) -> int
docs.text:
I append string content to the end of a file, creating it if it does not exist. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (file_append "log.txt" "New log entry\n")

docs.title: file_remove(path: string) -> int
docs.text:
I delete a file permanently. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (file_remove "temp.txt")

docs.title: file_rename(old_path: string, new_path: string) -> int
docs.text:
I rename or move a file. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (file_rename "old.txt" "new.txt")

docs.title: file_exists(path: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the file exists and is accessible.

if (file_exists "config.json") {
    let config: string = (file_read "config.json")
} else {
    (println "Config file not found")
}

docs.title: file_size(path: string) -> int
docs.text:
I return the file size in bytes, or -1 on error.

let size: int = (file_size "data.bin")
(println (str_concat "File size: " (int_to_string size)))

docs.title: dir_exists(path: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the path exists and is a directory.

if (not (dir_exists "output")) {
    (dir_create "output")
}

docs.title: dir_create(path: string) -> int
docs.text:
I create a directory. Parent directories must already exist. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (dir_create "build/output")

docs.title: dir_remove(path: string) -> int
docs.text:
I remove an empty directory. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (dir_remove "temp")

docs.title: dir_list(path: string) -> array<string>
docs.text:
I list all entries in a directory and return an array of filenames (not full paths). I return an empty array on error.

let entries: array<string> = (dir_list ".")
for entry in entries {
    (println entry)
}

docs.title: getcwd() -> string
docs.text:
I return the current working directory as an absolute path.

let cwd: string = (getcwd)
(println cwd)   # Prints e.g. "/home/user/project"

docs.title: chdir(path: string) -> int
docs.text:
I change the current working directory. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (chdir "/tmp")
let cwd: string = (getcwd)
(println cwd)   # Prints "/tmp"

docs.title: fs_walkdir(path: string) -> array<string>
docs.text:
I recursively walk a directory tree and return an array of all file paths found.

let files: array<string> = (fs_walkdir ".")
for f in files {
    (println f)
}

docs.title: tmp_dir() -> string
docs.text:
I return the system's temporary directory path.

let tmp: string = (tmp_dir)
(println tmp)   # Prints e.g. "/tmp"

docs.title: mktemp(prefix: string) -> string
docs.text:
I create a new temporary file with the given prefix and return its path. The file is created and left open for writing.

let path: string = (mktemp "nano_work_")
(file_write path "some data")

docs.title: mktemp_dir(prefix: string) -> string
docs.text:
I create a new temporary directory with the given prefix and return its path.

let dir: string = (mktemp_dir "nano_build_")
let out: string = (path_join dir "output.txt")

docs.title: path_isfile(path: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the path exists and is a regular file.

if (path_isfile "config.json") {
    (println "Found config file")
}

docs.title: path_isdir(path: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the path exists and is a directory.

if (path_isdir "src") {
    (println "src directory exists")
}

docs.title: path_join(a: string, b: string) -> string
docs.text:
I join two path components with the appropriate separator. I use / on Unix.

let full: string = (path_join "/home/user" "documents")
# Returns "/home/user/documents"
let nested: string = (path_join "src" "main.nano")
# Returns "src/main.nano"

docs.title: path_basename(path: string) -> string
docs.text:
I extract the filename component from a path.

(path_basename "/path/to/file.txt")   # Returns "file.txt"
(path_basename "src/main.nano")       # Returns "main.nano"

docs.title: path_dirname(path: string) -> string
docs.text:
I extract the directory component from a path.

(path_dirname "/path/to/file.txt")   # Returns "/path/to"
(path_dirname "src/main.nano")       # Returns "src"

docs.title: path_normalize(path: string) -> string
docs.text:
I normalize a path by resolving ., .., and redundant separators.

(path_normalize "./foo/../bar/./baz")   # Returns "bar/baz"
(path_normalize "/a//b/./c")           # Returns "/a/b/c"

docs.title: system(command: string) -> int
docs.text:
I execute a shell command and wait for it to complete. I return the exit code.

let status: int = (system "ls -la")
if (!= status 0) { (println "Command failed") }

docs.title: exit(code: int) -> void
docs.text:
I terminate the program immediately with the given exit code.

if (not (file_exists "required.txt")) {
    (println "Error: required.txt not found")
    (exit 1)
}

docs.title: getenv(name: string) -> string
docs.text:
I return the value of an environment variable. I return an empty string if it is not set.

let home: string = (getenv "HOME")
let path: string = (getenv "PATH")

docs.title: setenv(name: string, value: string) -> int
docs.text:
I set an environment variable for the current process and its children. I return 0 on success and 1 on failure.

let status: int = (setenv "MY_VAR" "my_value")

docs.title: process_run(command: string) -> array<string>
docs.text:
I execute a command and return its output as an array of strings. The first element is the exit code as a string, and subsequent elements are lines of stdout.

let result: array<string> = (process_run "echo hello")
let code: string = (at result 0)     # "0" (exit code)
let line: string = (at result 1)     # "hello"

docs.title: bytes_from_string(s: string) -> array<int>
docs.text:
I convert a string to an array of byte values (0–255), one per character.

let bytes: array<int> = (bytes_from_string "Hello")
(at bytes 0)   # Returns 72 ('H')

docs.title: string_from_bytes(bytes: array<int>) -> string
docs.text:
I construct a string from an array of byte values.

let bytes: array<int> = [72, 101, 108, 108, 111]
let s: string = (string_from_bytes bytes)   # Returns "Hello"

docs.title: bstr_utf8_length(s: string) -> int
docs.text:
I return the number of Unicode code points in a UTF-8 encoded string, which may differ from its byte length for multi-byte characters.

(bstr_utf8_length "Hello")   # Returns 5
(bstr_utf8_length "café")    # Returns 4 (4 code points, 5 bytes)

docs.title: bstr_utf8_char_at(s: string, index: int) -> int
docs.text:
I return the Unicode code point at the given character index (not byte index) in a UTF-8 string.

(bstr_utf8_char_at "Hello" 0)   # Returns 72 ('H')
(bstr_utf8_char_at "café" 3)    # Returns the code point for 'é'

docs.title: bstr_validate_utf8(s: string) -> bool
docs.text:
I return true if the string contains valid UTF-8 encoded text.

if (bstr_validate_utf8 content) {
    (println "Valid UTF-8")
} else {
    (println "Invalid encoding")
}

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
I am primarily an explicitly-typed language. I require you to provide most type annotations. I perform limited type inference in specific contexts to reduce verbosity. I do this only where it does not sacrifice clarity.

This document explains what I can and cannot infer. It will help you understand when I require you to be explicit.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Situation Inference Example Variable declarations Required let x: int = 42 Function parameters Required fn f(x: int) Function return types Required fn f() -> int Array element types Inferred from literal let arr: array<int> = [1, 2, 3] Empty array types Context-dependent Requires type annotation Struct literal names Inferred from context See below Generic type parameters Required let list: List<int> = ...

docs.title: What I Require You To Annotate
1. Variable Type Annotations
docs.text:
I require all variable declarations to include a type annotation.

# CORRECT
let x: int = 42
let name: string = "Alice"
let flag: bool = true
let pi: float = 3.14

# ERROR: Missing type annotation
let x = 42          # I will not compile this
let name = "Alice"  # I will not compile this

Explicit types improve code clarity. They make it easier for humans and machines to understand code without searching for context.

docs.title: 2. Function Parameter Types
docs.text:
I require all function parameters to have type annotations.

# CORRECT
fn add(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (+ a b)
}

# ERROR: Missing parameter types
fn add(a, b) -> int {  # I will not compile this
    return (+ a b)
}

docs.title: 3. Function Return Types
docs.text:
I require all functions to declare their return type. This includes functions that return void.

# CORRECT
fn get_value() -> int {
    return 42
}

fn print_message() -> void {
    (println "Hello")
}

# ERROR: Missing return type
fn get_value() {  # I will not compile this
    return 42
}

I do not require return types for shadow test blocks. They are always void.

docs.title: 4. Generic Type Parameters
docs.text:
I require explicit type parameters for generic types like List<T> and HashMap<K,V>.

# CORRECT
let numbers: List<int> = (List_int_new)
let mapping: HashMap<string, int> = (map_new)

# ERROR: Missing type parameters
let numbers: List = (List_int_new)     # I will not compile this
let mapping: HashMap = (map_new)       # I will not compile this

I use monomorphization to specialize code at compile time. I must know the concrete types to generate the correct implementation.

docs.title: 1. Array Element Types (From Literals)
docs.text:
When you provide an array literal with elements, I infer the element type from the literal itself.

# I verify that [1, 2, 3] matches array<int>
let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3]

# I determine the element type from the literal contents
let values: array<int> = [10, 20, 30]
let names: array<string> = ["Alice", "Bob"]

You must still provide the type annotation on the variable. I check that it matches the literal.

docs.title: 2. Empty Array Types (Context-Dependent)
docs.text:
Empty arrays [] require type information from the context.

# CORRECT: I use the variable annotation
let empty: array<int> = []

# CORRECT: I use the function parameter type
fn process_numbers(nums: array<int>) -> void {
    # ...
}
# I infer the type from the parameter when you call me
(process_numbers [])

# ERROR: I have no context for this empty array type
let unknown = []  # I will not compile this

docs.title: 3. Struct Literal Names (From Context)
docs.text:
When I know which struct type is expected, you can use anonymous struct literals by omitting the struct name.

This feature is present in my typechecker. It is useful for:

  1. Function return values
  2. Nested struct initialization
  3. Function call arguments
struct Point {
    x: int,
    y: int
}

# I accept the explicit struct name
fn create_point() -> Point {
    return Point { x: 10, y: 20 }
}

My typechecker has logic for anonymous struct literals. My parser and syntax currently require explicit struct names. I document this for completeness.

docs.title: Example 1: Array Type Inference
docs.text:
fn process_data() -> void {
    # I infer the element type from the literal
    let scores: array<int> = [95, 87, 92, 88]

    # I require an explicit type for empty arrays
    let mut results: array<int> = []

    # I ensure the type matches the literal
    let names: array<string> = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]
}

shadow process_data {
    (process_data)
    assert true
}

docs.title: Example 2: Generic Type Parameters
docs.text:
# I require explicit K,V parameters for HashMap
fn create_lookup() -> HashMap<string, int> {
    let map: HashMap<string, int> = (map_new)
    (map_insert map "answer" 42)
    return map
}

shadow create_lookup {
    let m: HashMap<string, int> = (create_lookup)
    assert true
}

# I require an explicit T parameter for List
fn create_list() -> List<int> {
    let list: List<int> = (List_int_new)
    (List_int_push list 1)
    (List_int_push list 2)
    return list
}

shadow create_list {
    let l: List<int> = (create_list)
    assert true
}

docs.title: Example 3: Variable Type Requirements
docs.text:
# I will not compile these variable declarations
# let x = 42
# let name = "Alice"
# let flag = (> 5 3)

# I require these versions
let x: int = 42
let name: string = "Alice"
let flag: bool = (> 5 3)

docs.title: Mistake 1: Omitting Variable Types
docs.text:
# ERROR
let count = 0

# CORRECT
let count: int = 0

I will produce this error: Error: Variable declaration requires explicit type annotation

docs.title: Mistake 2: Omitting Function Parameter Types
docs.text:
# ERROR
fn double(x) -> int {
    return (* x 2)
}

# CORRECT
fn double(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x 2)
}

shadow double {
    assert (== (double 5) 10)
}

docs.title: Mistake 3: Missing Generic Type Parameters
docs.text:
# ERROR
let numbers: List = (List_int_new)

# CORRECT
let numbers: List<int> = (List_int_new)

I will produce this error: Error: Generic type List requires type parameters

docs.title: Mistake 4: Empty Array Without Type
docs.text:
# ERROR
let mut results = []

# CORRECT
let mut results: array<int> = []

docs.title: Why I Am Explicit
docs.text:
My explicit typing philosophy serves these goals:

  1. Machine-Friendly. AI systems generate correct code without complex inference rules.
  2. Readability. Code is self-documenting. Types are always visible.
  3. Simplicity. Fewer inference rules mean less cognitive load for you.
  4. Clarity. Error messages are clearer when types are explicit.
  5. Speed. Less type inference results in faster compilation.

docs.title: Comparison With Other Languages
docs.text:
Language Variable Types Parameter Types Return Types NanoLang Explicit Explicit Explicit C Explicit Explicit Explicit Go Can omit (:=) Explicit Explicit Rust Can omit Can omit Can omit TypeScript Can omit Can omit Can infer Python Optional Optional Optional

I am closer to C in my explicitness. I am simpler than Rust or TypeScript.

docs.title: Future Considerations
docs.text:
My type inference system is intentionally minimal. I could add these features:

  1. Local variable inference. let x = 42
  2. Return type inference. fn f() { return 42 }
  3. Generic function parameters. fn identity<T>(x: T) -> T

I do not currently plan to add these features. They conflict with my goal of explicitness.

docs.title: 1. Always Annotate Variables
docs.text:
Include the annotation even when you think the type is obvious.

# Good. I find this clear and explicit.
let message: string = "Hello"
let count: int = 0
let ready: bool = false

docs.title: 2. Use Descriptive Types for Generics
docs.text:
# Good. Your type parameters are clear to me.
let user_ids: List<int> = (List_int_new)
let user_map: HashMap<string, int> = (map_new)

docs.title: 3. Use Function Signatures
docs.text:
Your function signatures serve as documentation for me and for you.

# Good. This signature tells me everything I need to know.
fn calculate_distance(x1: int, y1: int, x2: int, y2: int) -> float {
    return 0.0
}

shadow calculate_distance {
    let dist: float = (calculate_distance 0 0 3 4)
    assert true
}

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
I follow these principles for type inference:

  • Default. I require explicit type annotations.
  • Exception. I allow limited inference for array and struct literals.
  • Goal. I maximize clarity and machine-friendliness.

When you are in doubt, add the type annotation. I value explicitness over brevity.

See Also: - Type System Specification - Generics Deep Dive - Error Messages Guide - Getting Started

Last Updated: February 20, 2026

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
I provide UTF-8 string support through two complementary string types. I don't try to hide the underlying byte representation from you. - string - C-compatible UTF-8 strings. These are null-terminated char* pointers. - bstring - Binary strings with explicit length. I use these when I need to be UTF-8 aware or handle binary data.

This document explains what Unicode features I support, where I have limitations, and how I expect you to work with international text.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text: Feature string bstring Notes UTF-8 Storage Yes Yes Both store UTF-8 bytes Null-terminated Yes No string is C-compatible Embedded Nulls No Yes bstring allows \0 in data Length = Bytes Yes Yes Both count bytes, not characters Character Operations Limited Better bstring has UTF-8 helpers FFI Compatible Yes Partial string works directly with C Normalization No No I do not support this Grapheme Clusters No No I do not support this

docs.title: What is string?
docs.text:
My string type is a null-terminated C string (char*). I use it to store UTF-8 encoded text.

let message: string = "Hello, 世界!"  # UTF-8 bytes stored
let emoji: string = "Hello 👋"       # Multi-byte emoji supported

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
What I support: - Storing UTF-8 text as a sequence of bytes. - Passing strings to C FFI functions directly. - Concatenation through str_concat. - Comparison through str_equals. - Byte-length retrieval through str_length. - Substring creation by byte index through str_substring.

What I do not support: - Embedded null bytes. A \0 byte terminates the string. - Character counting. I only count bytes in str_length. - Unicode normalization. - Grapheme cluster operations.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
fn greet(name: string) -> string {
    return (str_concat "Hello, " name)
}

shadow greet {
    let msg: string = (greet "世界")
    assert (str_equals msg "Hello, 世界")

    # Length is in BYTES, not characters
    # "世界" is 6 bytes in UTF-8 (3 bytes × 2 characters)
    assert (== (str_length "世界") 6)
}

docs.title: When to use string
docs.text: I recommend using string when you: - Interface with C libraries. - Work with simple text that doesn't need character-level operations. - Do not need embedded null bytes. - Require maximum C compatibility.

docs.title: What is bstring?
docs.text:
I use the bstring type (nl_string_t*) when I need more control. - I store the length separately, so I don't rely on null termination. - I allow embedded null bytes. - I provide UTF-8 validation and character counting.

let data: bstring = (bstr_new "Hello, 世界!")
let bytes: int = (bstr_length data)       # Byte count
let chars: int = (bstr_utf8_length data)  # Character count

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
What I support: - Storing binary data with embedded null bytes. - UTF-8 validation through bstr_validate_utf8. - Character counting through bstr_utf8_length. - Byte-length retrieval through bstr_length. - Substring creation by byte index through bstr_substring. - Byte access through bstr_byte_at. - Conversion to and from string using bstr_to_cstr and bstr_new.

What I do not support: - Direct FFI use. You must convert to string first. - Unicode normalization. - Grapheme cluster operations. - Character-indexed substring operations.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
fn analyze_text(text: string) -> (int, int) {
    let bs: bstring = (bstr_new text)
    let byte_count: int = (bstr_length bs)
    let char_count: int = (bstr_utf8_length bs)
    return (byte_count, char_count)
}

shadow analyze_text {
    # ASCII: 1 byte per character
    let ascii: (int, int) = (analyze_text "Hello")
    assert (== ascii.0 5)  # 5 bytes
    assert (== ascii.1 5)  # 5 characters

    # Multi-byte UTF-8: "世界" = 6 bytes, 2 characters
    let utf8: (int, int) = (analyze_text "世界")
    assert (== utf8.0 6)   # 6 bytes
    assert (== utf8.1 2)   # 2 characters

    # Emoji: "👋" = 4 bytes, 1 character
    let emoji: (int, int) = (analyze_text "👋")
    assert (== emoji.0 4)  # 4 bytes
    assert (== emoji.1 1)  # 1 character
}

docs.title: When to use bstring
docs.text: I recommend using bstring when you: - Need to count characters rather than just bytes. - Work with binary data or embedded nulls. - Need to validate UTF-8 sequences. - Do not need direct C FFI compatibility.

docs.title: Indexing is byte-based
docs.text:
I use byte indices for both string and bstring. I do not use character indices.

fn demonstrate_byte_indexing() -> void {
    let text: string = "Hi👋"  # H=1 byte, i=1 byte, 👋=4 bytes

    # str_substring works with BYTE indices
    let hi: string = (str_substring text 0 2)    # "Hi" (bytes 0-1)
    let wave: string = (str_substring text 2 6)  # "👋" (bytes 2-5)

    # char_at returns byte at index (as ASCII value)
    let h: int = (char_at text 0)  # 72 (ASCII 'H')
    let i: int = (char_at text 1)  # 105 (ASCII 'i')
    # (char_at text 2) returns first byte of 👋, not the full character!
}

shadow demonstrate_byte_indexing {
    (demonstrate_byte_indexing)
    assert true
}

docs.title: Character counting
docs.text:
If you want to count characters, use my bstr_utf8_length function.

fn count_characters(text: string) -> int {
    let bs: bstring = (bstr_new text)
    return (bstr_utf8_length bs)
}

shadow count_characters {
    assert (== (count_characters "Hello") 5)
    assert (== (count_characters "世界") 2)
    assert (== (count_characters "👋🌍") 2)  # 2 emoji = 2 characters
}

docs.title: What I do not support
docs.text:
I am honest about what I cannot do. I do not support:

  1. Unicode Normalization (NFC, NFD, NFKC, NFKD). I treat "é" as one character and "e" with a combining accent as two different byte sequences. I do not perform automatic normalization.
  2. Grapheme Cluster Operations. A family emoji like "👨‍👩‍👧‍👦" consists of multiple codepoints. My bstr_utf8_length function counts these codepoints. It does not count grapheme clusters as a single unit.
  3. Character-indexed Substrings. All my substring operations use byte indices. You must calculate byte offsets yourself if you work with multi-byte characters.
  4. Unicode Case Folding. My char_to_upper and char_to_lower functions only work for ASCII characters. I do not support Turkish İ or German ß.
  5. Bidirectional Text (BiDi). I do not support right-to-left languages like Arabic or Hebrew. I render text exactly as it is stored.

docs.title: Examples of my limitations
docs.text:
fn demonstrate_limitations() -> void {
    # Normalization: These are DIFFERENT
    let nfc: string = "é"          # Single character (U+00E9)
    let nfd: string = "é"          # e + combining accent (U+0065 U+0301)
    assert (not (str_equals nfc nfd))  # They're different byte sequences!

    # Grapheme clusters: Family emoji counted as multiple
    let family: bstring = (bstr_new "👨‍👩‍👧‍👦")
    let count: int = (bstr_utf8_length family)
    # count is 7 (Man + ZWJ + Woman + ZWJ + Girl + ZWJ + Boy)
    # NOT 1 as a user might expect!
}

shadow demonstrate_limitations {
    (demonstrate_limitations)
    assert true
}

docs.title: 1. Use bstring for character counting
docs.text:
# I recommend this for accurate character counts.
fn count_visual_length(text: string) -> int {
    let bs: bstring = (bstr_new text)
    return (bstr_utf8_length bs)
}

# I do not recommend this. It counts bytes, not characters.
fn count_visual_length_wrong(text: string) -> int {
    return (str_length text)  # Wrong for multi-byte characters!
}

docs.title: 2. Validate UTF-8 input
docs.text:
fn safe_process(text: string) -> bool {
    let bs: bstring = (bstr_new text)
    if (not (bstr_validate_utf8 bs)) {
        (println "Invalid UTF-8!")
        return false
    }
    # Process valid UTF-8...
    return true
}

shadow safe_process {
    assert (safe_process "Hello 世界")
    assert true
}

docs.title: 3. Be careful with byte indexing
docs.text:
# This is safe because it processes the entire string.
fn uppercase_ascii(text: string) -> string {
    let mut result: string = ""
    let len: int = (str_length text)
    let mut i: int = 0

    while (< i len) {
        let c: int = (char_at text i)
        if (and (>= c 97) (<= c 122)) {  # a-z
            set c (- c 32)  # Convert to uppercase
        }
        set result (str_concat result (string_from_char c))
        set i (+ i 1)
    }
    return result
}

# This is dangerous. Substring at an arbitrary byte index might split a character.
fn bad_substring(text: string, start: int, end: int) -> string {
    return (str_substring text start end)
}

docs.title: 4. Use string for FFI and convert when necessary
docs.text:
# My FFI functions expect C strings.
extern fn write_file(path: string, content: string) -> int

fn save_data(path: string, data: bstring) -> int {
    # I convert bstring to string for FFI calls.
    let content: string = (bstr_to_cstr data)
    return (write_file path content)
}

shadow save_data {
    let data: bstring = (bstr_new "Hello")
    assert true
}

docs.title: Working with emoji
docs.text:
fn count_emoji(text: string) -> int {
    # I count codepoints here, not visual characters.
    let bs: bstring = (bstr_new text)
    return (bstr_utf8_length bs)
}

shadow count_emoji {
    # Simple emoji: 1 codepoint
    assert (== (count_emoji "😀") 1)

    # Skin tone modifier: 2 codepoints
    assert (== (count_emoji "👋🏽") 2)  # Wave + skin tone

    # Combined emoji: multiple codepoints
    let family_count: int = (count_emoji "👨‍👩‍👧‍👦")
    # I count 7 codepoints here, not 1.
}

docs.title: Internationalization
docs.text:
fn greet_multilingual(lang: string, name: string) -> string {
    if (str_equals lang "en") {
        return (str_concat "Hello, " name)
    } else if (str_equals lang "zh") {
        return (str_concat "你好，" name)
    } else if (str_equals lang "ar") {
        return (str_concat "مرحبا، " name)  # I do not support BiDi
    } else {
        return (str_concat "Hi, " name)
    }
}

shadow greet_multilingual {
    assert (str_equals (greet_multilingual "en" "Alice") "Hello, Alice")
    assert (str_equals (greet_multilingual "zh" "小明") "你好，小明")
}

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Task My Recommended Approach Store UTF-8 text Use string for simplicity or bstring for advanced needs Count characters Use bstr_utf8_length Count bytes Use str_length or bstr_length Create substring by bytes Use str_substring or bstr_substring Use C libraries (FFI) Use string. Convert from bstring if needed Validate UTF-8 Use bstr_validate_utf8 Handle embedded nulls Use bstring Case conversion ASCII only using char_to_upper or char_to_lower Normalization I do not support this. Handle it externally Grapheme clusters I do not support this. I count codepoints
