docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Track function purity and side effects at the type level (IO monad style).

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
No way to distinguish pure from impure functions:

fn pure_math(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x x)  // Pure: no side effects
}

fn impure_io(x: int) -> int {
    (println x)  // Impure: I/O side effect!
    return x
}

// Both have same type: fn(int) -> int
// Caller can't know if function does I/O!

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Add effect annotations to track purity:

fn pure_math(x: int) -> int @pure {
    return (* x x)  // OK: no effects
}

fn impure_io(x: int) -> int @io {
    (println x)  // OK: @io allows I/O
    return x
}

fn needs_pure(f: fn(int) -> int @pure) -> int {
    return (f 42)  // Only accepts pure functions
}

// ERROR: Can't pass @io function where @pure expected
let result: int = (needs_pure impure_io)

docs.title: Effect Hierarchy
docs.text:
@pure      // No effects at all
@read      // Reads external state (files, network)
@write     // Writes external state  
@io        // Both read and write
@unsafe    // Can crash, undefined behavior

Subtyping: @pure <: @read <: @io, @pure <: @write <: @io

docs.title: Option A: Annotation-Based (Simpler)
docs.text:
// Explicit annotations
fn map(arr: array<int>, f: fn(int) -> int @pure) -> array<int> @pure {
    // Implementation must be pure
}

// Type error if calling impure function
let result: array<int> = (map numbers impure_io)  // ERROR

docs.title: Option B: Inferred Effects (Advanced)
docs.text:
// Compiler infers effects
fn compute(x: int) -> int {  // Inferred: @pure
    return (+ x 1)
}

fn log_and_compute(x: int) -> int {  // Inferred: @io
    (println x)
    return (+ x 1)
}

docs.title: Option C: Monadic (Haskell-style)
docs.text:
// IO monad separates pure and impure
fn pure_compute(x: int) -> int {  // Pure
    return (+ x 1)
}

fn io_action(x: int) -> IO<int> {  // Returns IO action
    do IO {
        (println x)
        return (+ x 1)
    }
}

fn main() -> int {
    let result: int = (run_io (io_action 42))  // Execute IO
    return result
}

docs.title: Phase 1: Effect Annotations (20 hours)
docs.text:
1. Add effect keywords: @pure, @io, @read, @write, @unsafe
  2. Parse annotations: Extend function syntax
  3. Store in AST: Add effect field to FunctionSignature
  4. Type check: Verify function body matches annotation
// Lexer: Add tokens
TOKEN_AT_PURE
TOKEN_AT_IO
TOKEN_AT_READ
TOKEN_AT_WRITE
TOKEN_AT_UNSAFE

// Parser: Parse effect annotations
fn parse_effect_annotation() -> Effect

// TypeChecker: Verify effect compliance
fn check_function_effect(fn_sig: FunctionSignature, body: ASTNode) -> bool

docs.title: Phase 2: Effect Inference (25 hours)
docs.text:
1. Infer effects: Bottom-up from expressions
  2. Propagate through calls: Track transitive effects
  3. Default to safe: Assume @pure unless proven otherwise
// Inference rules
pure_expr → @pure
(println x) → @io
(+ a b) → @pure
(f x) where f: @io → @io  // Transitivity

docs.title: Phase 3: Effect Polymorphism (20 hours)
docs.text:
// Effect-polymorphic map
fn map<E>(arr: array<int>, f: fn(int) -> int @E) -> array<int> @E {
    // Effect E propagates through
}

// Specialized to pure
let pure_result: array<int> = (map nums pure_fn)  // @pure

// Specialized to io  
let io_result: array<int> = (map nums io_fn)  // @io

docs.title: Phase 4: Effect Handlers (40 hours)
docs.text:
Advanced: Algebraic effects and handlers

effect State<S> {
    get: () -> S
    set: (S) -> ()
}

fn counter() -> int with State<int> {
    let current: int = (effect.get)
    (effect.set (+ current 1))
    return current
}

fn run_with_state<S, A>(init: S, action: () -> A with State<S>) -> A {
    // Handler implementation
}

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text:
Major breaking change: All existing functions need effect annotations.

Migration Strategy:

  1. Phase 1: Warnings only (--warn-effects)
  2. Phase 2: Default to @io (permissive)
  3. Phase 3: Require explicit annotations (strict)
// Auto-migration tool
nanoc --infer-effects old.nano > new.nano

// Before
fn compute(x: int) -> int {
    return (+ x 1)
}

// After (inferred)
fn compute(x: int) -> int @pure {
    return (+ x 1)
}

docs.title: Standard Library Impact
docs.text:
All stdlib functions need effects:

// Math (pure)
fn add(a: int, b: int) -> int @pure { ... }
fn sqrt(x: float) -> float @pure { ... }

// I/O (io)
fn println(s: string) -> void @io { ... }
fn read_file(path: string) -> string @io { ... }

// Unsafe (unsafe)
extern fn malloc(size: int) -> ptr @unsafe

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
1. Effect checking is compile-time: Zero runtime overhead
  2. Monomorphization: Effect-polymorphic functions duplicated per effect
  3. Optimization: Pure functions can be memoized, reordered

docs.title: With Checked Arithmetic
docs.text:
fn safe_add(a: int, b: int) -> Result<int, string> @pure {
    return (checked_add a b)  // Pure: no I/O
}

docs.title: With Unsafe Blocks
docs.text:
fn ffi_call() -> int @unsafe {
    unsafe {
        return (external_c_function)  // Marked unsafe
    }
}

docs.title: With Totality Checker
docs.text:
fn total_and_pure(x: int) -> int @pure @total {
    return (+ x 1)  // Provably total AND pure
}

docs.title: Estimated Effort
docs.text:
• Phase 1 (Annotations): 20 hours
  • Phase 2 (Inference): 25 hours
  • Phase 3 (Polymorphism): 20 hours
  • Phase 4 (Handlers): 40 hours
  • Stdlib Migration: 10 hours
  • Documentation: 5 hours
  • Testing: 10 hours
  • TOTAL: ~130 hours (3+ weeks)

Dual-Impl Overhead: 1.8x (C + NanoLang typecheckers)

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Haskell IO Monad: https://wiki.haskell.org/IO_inside
  • Koka Effects: https://koka-lang.github.io/koka/doc/book.html
  • OCaml Effects: https://ocaml.org/manual/effects.html
  • Eff Language: https://www.eff-lang.org/
  • Frank: https://arxiv.org/abs/1611.09259

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
🟡 PLANNED - Requires major type system extension

Recommendation: - Too large for immediate implementation - Consider lightweight @pure annotation first (Phase 1 only) - Full effect system is a v2.0 feature

Alternatives: - Document pure/impure in comments - Use naming conventions (io_read_file vs pure_compute) - Wait for community demand

Related: nanolang-yq92

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add Rust-style ? operator to unwrap Result<T,E> or early-return errors.

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
Error handling is verbose:

fn parse_and_double(s: string) -> Result<int, string> {
    let r1: Result<int, string> = (str_to_int s)
    match r1 {
        Ok(v) => {
            let doubled: int = (* v.value 2)
            return Result.Ok { value: doubled }
        }
        Error(e) => {
            return Result.Error { error: e.error }
        }
    }
}

With ? operator:

fn parse_and_double(s: string) -> Result<int, string> {
    let num: int = (str_to_int s)?  // Unwrap or early return
    return Result.Ok { value: (* num 2) }
}

docs.title: Syntax Design
docs.text:
// Postfix ? operator
let value: T = (result_expr)?

// Desugars to:
let temp_result: Result<T, E> = (result_expr)
match temp_result {
    Ok(v) => v.value,
    Error(e) => return Result.Error { error: e.error }
}

docs.title: Simple Error Propagation
docs.text:
fn read_and_parse(path: string) -> Result<int, string> {
    let contents: string = (read_file path)?
    let num: int = (str_to_int contents)?
    return Result.Ok { value: num }
}

docs.title: Chaining Multiple Operations
docs.text:
fn complex_operation(x: int) -> Result<int, string> {
    let a: int = (checked_add x 10)?
    let b: int = (checked_mul a 2)?
    let c: int = (checked_div b 3)?
    return Result.Ok { value: c }
}

docs.title: Error Context
docs.text:
fn parse_config(path: string) -> Result<Config, string> {
    let json: string = (read_file path)?
        .map_err("Failed to read config")?
    let config: Config = (parse_json json)?
        .map_err("Failed to parse config")?
    return Result.Ok { value: config }
}

docs.title: Phase 1: Lexer (1 hour)
docs.text:
// Add QUESTION token
enum TokenType {
    // ... existing ...
    TOKEN_QUESTION = 68  // After TOKEN_UNSAFE
}

// Lexer rule
if (c == '?') {
    return Token { token_type: TOKEN_QUESTION, ... }
}

docs.title: Phase 2: Parser (3 hours)
docs.text:
// Parse postfix ? operator
fn parse_postfix_expr(base: ASTNode) -> ASTNode {
    if (current_token == TOKEN_QUESTION) {
        consume(TOKEN_QUESTION)
        return AST_TRY_UNWRAP { expr: base }
    }
    return base
}

// AST node
enum ASTNodeType {
    // ... existing ...
    AST_TRY_UNWRAP  // expr?
}

struct ASTTryUnwrap {
    node_type: int,
    line: int,
    column: int,
    expr: int  // Expression that returns Result<T,E>
}

docs.title: Phase 3: Type Checker (5 hours)
docs.text:
fn check_try_unwrap(tc: TypeChecker, node: ASTTryUnwrap) -> Type {
    // 1. Check expr type
    let expr_type: Type = (check_expr tc node.expr)

    // 2. Verify it's Result<T, E>
    if (!= expr_type.kind TypeKind.TYPE_GENERIC) {
        error("? operator requires Result<T,E> type")
    }
    if (!= expr_type.name "Result") {
        error("? operator requires Result<T,E> type")
    }

    // 3. Verify function returns Result<_, E> (same error type)
    let fn_ret_type: Type = (current_function_return_type tc)
    if (!= fn_ret_type.kind TypeKind.TYPE_GENERIC) {
        error("? operator requires function to return Result<_,E>")
    }

    // 4. Verify error types match
    let expr_err_type: Type = (type_param_at expr_type 1)
    let fn_err_type: Type = (type_param_at fn_ret_type 1)
    if (not (types_compatible expr_err_type fn_err_type)) {
        error("Error type mismatch")
    }

    // 5. Extract success type T
    return (type_param_at expr_type 0)
}

docs.title: Phase 4: Transpiler (6 hours)
docs.text:
// Desugar ? operator to match expression
fn transpile_try_unwrap(t: Transpiler, node: ASTTryUnwrap) -> string {
    // Generate unique temp variable
    let temp_var: string = (gen_temp_var t)

    // Build match expression
    let result: StringBuilder = (sb_new)
    (sb_append result "({ ")

    // Evaluate expression into temp
    (sb_append result (type_to_c_type (get_expr_type node.expr)))
    (sb_append result " ")
    (sb_append result temp_var)
    (sb_append result " = ")
    (sb_append result (transpile_expr t node.expr))
    (sb_append result "; ")

    // Match on result
    (sb_append result "if (")
    (sb_append result temp_var)
    (sb_append result ".tag == RESULT_OK) { ")
    (sb_append result temp_var)
    (sb_append result ".as.ok.value")
    (sb_append result " } else { return ")
    (sb_append result temp_var)  // Return error
    (sb_append result "; } })")

    return (sb_to_string result)
}

docs.title: Phase 5: Testing (2 hours)
docs.text:
// Test basic unwrap
shadow test_try_basic {
    fn ok_case() -> Result<int, string> {
        let r: Result<int, string> = Result.Ok { value: 42 }
        let v: int = r?
        return Result.Ok { value: v }
    }

    match (ok_case) {
        Ok(v) => assert (== v.value 42),
        Error(e) => assert false
    }
}

// Test error propagation
shadow test_try_error {
    fn error_case() -> Result<int, string> {
        let r: Result<int, string> = Result.Error { error: "fail" }
        let v: int = r?  // Should return early
        return Result.Ok { value: v }
    }

    match (error_case) {
        Ok(v) => assert false,
        Error(e) => assert (str_equals e.error "fail")
    }
}

// Test chaining
shadow test_try_chain {
    fn chain() -> Result<int, string> {
        let a: int = (checked_add 5 10)?
        let b: int = (checked_mul a 2)?
        return Result.Ok { value: b }
    }

    match (chain) {
        Ok(v) => assert (== v.value 30),
        Error(e) => assert false
    }
}

docs.title: Phase 6: Documentation (2 hours)
docs.text:

  • Add MEMORY.md section on error propagation
  • Create examples/nl_try_operator_demo.nano
  • Update CONTRIBUTING.md with pattern

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text: None - This is a pure addition, opt-in feature.

docs.title: With Checked Arithmetic
docs.text:
fn safe_compute(a: int, b: int, c: int) -> Result<int, string> {
    let sum: int = (checked_add a b)?
    let product: int = (checked_mul sum c)?
    return Result.Ok { value: product }
}

docs.title: With File I/O
docs.text:
fn process_file(path: string) -> Result<int, string> {
    let contents: string = (read_file path)?
    let lines: array<string> = (str_split contents "\n")?
    return Result.Ok { value: (array_length lines) }
}

docs.title: Estimated Effort
docs.text:
• Lexer: 1 hour
  • Parser: 3 hours (C + Nano)
  • Type Checker: 5 hours (C + Nano)
  • Transpiler: 6 hours (C + Nano)
  • Testing: 2 hours
  • Documentation: 2 hours
  • Examples: 1 hour
  • TOTAL: ~20 hours

Dual-Impl Overhead: 1.67x (most work is algorithmic, not typing)

docs.title: Comparison with Other Languages
docs.text: Language Syntax Notes Rust let x = expr?; Unwraps Result/Option Swift let x = try expr Throws on error Kotlin val x = expr!! Panics on null Zig const x = try expr; Error union NanoLang let x: int = expr? Unwraps Result

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
🟢 READY FOR IMPLEMENTATION - Well-defined, small scope

Recommendation: - High value (dramatically improves ergonomics) - Low cost (20 hours) - No breaking changes - Fits existing patterns

Priority: HIGH - Should be implemented soon

Related: nanolang-nt2j

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Verify all functions are total (always terminate, no runtime crashes possible).

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
Partial functions can cause runtime failures: 

fn head(arr: array<int>) -> int {
    return (at arr 0)  // CRASHES if arr is empty!
}

fn divide(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (/ a b)  // CRASHES if b == 0!
}

A totality checker would prove these functions can't crash or force defensive coding.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text: Add static analysis pass that checks: 1. Pattern Match Exhaustiveness: All union variants covered 2. Array Bounds: Provably in-bounds access 3. Division by Zero: Divisor provably non-zero 4. Recursion Termination: Structural recursion or explicit proof 5. Infinite Loops: While loops have termination proof

docs.title: Option A: Conservative (Recommended for v1)
docs.text:
Approach: Flag potentially partial functions, require explicit Result 

// Before (partial, rejected by checker)
fn head(arr: array<int>) -> int {
    return (at arr 0)  // ERROR: Unproven array access
}

// After (total, passes checker)
fn head(arr: array<int>) -> Result<int, string> {
    if (== (array_length arr) 0) {
        return Result.Error { error: "Empty array" }
    } else {
        return Result.Ok { value: (at arr 0) }
    }
}

Pros: - Simple to implement - Forces defensive programming - Clear error handling

Cons: - More verbose code - Requires Result everywhere

docs.title: Option B: Dependent Types
docs.text:
Approach: Non-empty array type array<T, n> where n > 0

// Type-level proof
fn head(arr: array<int, n>) -> int where (> n 0) {
    return (at arr 0)  // Provably safe!
}

Pros: - Elegant, compile-time proof - No runtime checks needed

Cons: - Major type system extension - Difficult to implement (~100+ hours) - Affects all array code

docs.title: Option C: Hybrid (Pragmatic)
docs.text:
Approach: Checker + opt-in dependent types

// Option 1: Use Result (conservative)
fn safe_head(arr: array<int>) -> Result<int, string> { ... }

// Option 2: Use assertion (checked)
@requires(not_empty(arr))
fn head(arr: array<int>) -> int {
    return (at arr 0)  // Checked by totality checker
}

// Option 3: Use dependent type (advanced)
fn head_proven(arr: array<int, n>) -> int where (> n 0) {
    return (at arr 0)  // Type-level proof
}

docs.title: Phase 1: Pattern Match Exhaustiveness (Core, 12 hours)
docs.text:
Already have: Warning for non-exhaustive matches Upgrade to: Error (make mandatory)

union Status { Ok, Error, Pending }

fn handle(s: Status) -> int {
    match s {
        Ok(v) => return 1,
        Error(e) => return 0
        // MISSING: Pending
    }
}
// ERROR: Non-exhaustive match, function is partial

Implementation: 1. Enhance existing match exhaustiveness check 2. Make it an error instead of warning 3. Add quick-fix: generate missing cases

docs.title: Phase 2: Simple Termination Analysis (20 hours)
docs.text:
Check: Structural recursion on lists

fn sum(arr: array<int>) -> int {
    if (== (array_length arr) 0) {
        return 0
    } else {
        let head: int = (at arr 0)
        let tail: array<int> = (array_slice arr 1 (array_length arr))
        return (+ head (sum tail))  // OK: Recursive call on smaller input
    }
}

Implementation: 1. Track recursive calls 2. Verify argument size decreases 3. Flag non-decreasing recursion

docs.title: Phase 3: Range Analysis (25 hours)
docs.text:
Check: Array bounds are proven in-range

fn get_first_three(arr: array<int>) -> array<int> {
    if (< (array_length arr) 3) {
        return []
    }
    // Checker knows: array_length arr >= 3
    let a: int = (at arr 0)  // Provably safe
    let b: int = (at arr 1)  // Provably safe
    let c: int = (at arr 2)  // Provably safe
    return [a, b, c]
}

Implementation: 1. Track constraints from if statements 2. Propagate bounds through blocks 3. Verify array accesses against bounds 4. Handle loop invariants

docs.title: Phase 4: Division-by-Zero (8 hours)
docs.text:
Check: Divisor is provably non-zero

fn safe_ratio(a: int, b: int) -> Result<int, string> {
    if (== b 0) {
        return Result.Error { error: "Division by zero" }
    }
    // Checker knows: b != 0
    return Result.Ok { value: (/ a b) }  // Provably safe
}

Implementation: 1. Track zero/non-zero constraints 2. Verify at division points 3. Flag unproven divisions

docs.title: Totality Annotation
docs.text:
@total  // Promise to checker
fn safe_function(x: int) -> int {
    return (+ x 1)  // Obviously total
}

shadow safe_function {
    assert (== (safe_function 5) 6)
}

docs.title: Partiality Warning
docs.text:
@partial  // Acknowledge partiality
fn dangerous_function(x: int) -> int {
    if (== x 0) {
        # Infinite loop
        let mut i: int = 0
        while true {
            set i (+ i 1)
        }
    }
    return x
}

docs.title: Checked Arithmetic
docs.text:
Use checked arithmetic to prove no overflow: 

fn add_checked(a: int, b: int) -> Result<int, string> {
    return (checked_add a b)  // Total: always returns
}

docs.title: Unsafe Blocks
docs.text:
Totality checker skips unsafe blocks: 

fn call_ffi() -> int {
    unsafe {
        return (some_c_function)  // Unchecked
    }
}

docs.title: Minimal (Phase 1 only)
docs.text:
• 12 hours: Pattern match exhaustiveness
  • Benefit: Eliminates partial matches
  • Limitation: Doesn't check array bounds, recursion

docs.title: Moderate (Phases 1-2)
docs.text:
• 32 hours: + Structural recursion
  • Benefit: Proves recursive functions terminate
  • Limitation: No array bounds checking

docs.title: Full (All Phases)
docs.text:
• 65 hours: Complete totality checker
  • Benefit: Provably total functions
  • Limitation: May flag some safe code (false positives)

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
Breaking Change: No Opt-in: Yes (via @total annotation or compiler flag) Migration: Existing code unaffected unless --strict-totality

nanoc --strict-totality file.nano  # Enforce totality
nanoc file.nano                     # Warnings only

docs.text:

  • Agda: https://wiki.portal.chalmers.se/agda/
  • Coq: https://coq.inria.fr/
  • Liquid Haskell: https://ucsd-progsys.github.io/liquidhaskell/
  • CompCert: http://compcert.inria.fr/

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
🟡 PLANNED - Comprehensive design complete

Recommendation: Start with Phase 1 (pattern exhaustiveness) as it's high-value, low-effort.

Next Steps: 1. Implement mandatory exhaustiveness checking 2. Add @total/@partial annotations 3. Implement structural recursion checking 4. Consider range analysis for arrays

Related: nanolang-ygm9

docs.title: NanoLang RFCs
docs.text: This directory contains RFCs (Requests for Comments) for NanoLang language evolution.

docs.title: What is an RFC?
docs.text: An RFC is a formal proposal for a significant change to NanoLang. The RFC process ensures: - Community input on major decisions - Thorough design before implementation - Documentation of design rationale - Transparent decision-making

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
rfcs/
├── README.md              # This file
├── 0000-template.md       # Template for new RFCs
├── accepted/              # Accepted RFCs
├── rejected/              # Rejected RFCs (with reasons)
└── postponed/             # Postponed RFCs (good ideas, wrong time)

Quick start: 1. Copy 0000-template.md to 0000-my-feature.md 2. Fill out template 3. Submit PR with title "RFC: My Feature" 4. Label PR with T-rfc 5. Engage with discussion

• file_read(path: string) -> string
  • file_write(path: string, content: string) -> int

docs.title: file_read(path: string) -> string { #file_read }
docs.text:
Reads the entire contents of a file and returns it as a string.

Parameters:

  • path — Path to the file to read.

Returns: File contents as a string, or empty string on error.

Example:

let content: string = (file_read "data.txt")
(println content)

docs.title: file_write(path: string, content: string) -> int { #file_write }
docs.text:
Writes a string to a file, overwriting any existing content.

Parameters:

  • path — Path to the file to write.
  • content — String content to write.

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error.

Example:

assert (== (file_write "output.txt" "Hello, World!\n") 0)

docs.title: file_append(path: string, content: string) -> int { #file_append }
docs.text:
Appends a string to the end of a file, creating it if it does not exist.

Parameters:

  • path — Path to the file to append to.
  • content — String content to append.

Returns: 0 on success, -1 on error.

Example:

(file_append "log.txt" "New log entry\n")

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
• abs(x: int | float) -> int | float
  • min(a: int | float, b: int | float) -> int | float
  • max(a: int | float, b: int | float) -> int | float
  • sqrt(x: int | float) -> float

docs.title: abs(x: int | float) -> int | float { #abs }
docs.text:
Returns the absolute value of a number. Works with both int and float.

Parameters:

  • x — The numeric value to take the absolute value of.

Returns: The non-negative magnitude of x.

Example:

(abs -5)      # => 5
(abs 3.14)    # => 3.14
(abs 0)       # => 0

docs.title: min(a: int | float, b: int | float) -> int | float { #min }
docs.text:
Returns the smaller of two numeric values. Both arguments must be the same type.

Parameters:

  • a — First numeric value.
  • b — Second numeric value.

Returns: The lesser of a and b.

Example:

(min 5 10)    # => 5
(min -3 0)    # => -3
(min 2.5 1.5) # => 1.5

docs.title: max(a: int | float, b: int | float) -> int | float { #max }
docs.text:
Returns the larger of two numeric values. Both arguments must be the same type.

Parameters:

  • a — First numeric value.
  • b — Second numeric value.

Returns: The greater of a and b.

Example:

(max 5 10)    # => 10
(max -3 0)    # => 0
(max 2.5 1.5) # => 2.5

docs.title: sqrt(x: int | float) -> float { #sqrt }
docs.text:
Returns the square root of a number. Accepts both int and float; always returns float.

Parameters:

  • x — A non-negative numeric value.

Returns: The square root of x as a float.

Example:

(sqrt 4.0)   # => 2.0
(sqrt 9)     # => 3.0
(sqrt 2.0)   # => 1.4142135623730951

docs.title: pow(base: int | float, exp: int | float) -> float { #pow }
docs.text:
Raises base to the power of exp. Always returns a float.

Parameters:

  • base — The base value.
  • exp — The exponent.

Returns: base raised to the power exp.

Example:

(pow 2.0 10.0)  # => 1024.0
(pow 3 2)       # => 9.0
(pow 2.0 0.5)   # => 1.4142135623730951

docs.title: str_length(s: string) -> int { #str_length }
docs.text:
Returns the number of characters in a string.

Parameters:

  • s — The string to measure.

Returns: The character count of s.

Example:

(str_length "hello")  # => 5
(str_length "")       # => 0

docs.title: str_concat(a: string, b: string) -> string { #str_concat }
docs.text:
Concatenates two strings and returns the combined result.

Parameters:

  • a — The first string.
  • b — The second string to append to a.

Returns: A new string containing a followed by b.

Example:

(str_concat "Hello, " "World!")  # => "Hello, World!"
(str_concat "" "abc")            # => "abc"

docs.title: str_substring(s: string, start: int, length: int) -> string { #str_substring }
docs.text:
Returns a substring of s beginning at start with the given length.

Parameters:

  • s — The source string.
  • start — Zero-based start index.
  • length — Number of characters to extract.

Returns: The extracted substring.

Example:

(str_substring "Hello, World!" 0 5)  # => "Hello"
(str_substring "Hello, World!" 7 5)  # => "World"

docs.title: Nanolang Tutorials
docs.text: Comprehensive step-by-step guides to master nanolang from beginner to advanced.

docs.title: 🌟 Beginner (Start Here!)
docs.text:
1. Getting Started ⏱️ 30 mins - Installation and setup - Your first program - Understanding prefix and infix notation - Basic syntax and types - Shadow tests introduction

2. Language Fundamentals ⏱️ 1-2 hours - Structs and enums - Pattern matching - Generic types - Higher-order functions - Error handling with unions

3. Module System ⏱️ 45 mins - Importing modules - Standard library overview - Creating your own modules - Module organization - Visibility and encapsulation

docs.title: 🔧 Intermediate
docs.text:
4. FFI Integration ⏱️ 1 hour Coming soon - See FFI Guide - Calling C libraries - Type marshaling - Creating C modules - Memory management in FFI

5. Building Applications ⏱️ 2-3 hours Coming soon - Project structure - Testing strategies - Database integration - File I/O patterns - Error handling patterns

docs.title: 🚀 Advanced
docs.text:
6. Performance Optimization ⏱️ 1-2 hours Coming soon - Benchmarking - Memory optimization - Compilation flags - Profiling tools

7. Advanced Type System ⏱️ 1 hour Coming soon - Complex generic constraints - Variance and type bounds - Phantom types - Type-level programming

docs.title: Quick Start for Impatient Developers
docs.text:
Already know programming? Start here:

# Install
git clone https://github.com/jordanhubbard/nanolang.git
cd nanolang && make

# Hello World
echo 'fn main() -> int {
    (println "Hello, World!")
    return 0
}' > hello.nano

./bin/nano hello.nano
./bin/nanoc hello.nano -o hello && ./hello

# Learn by example
cd examples && ls *.nano

Key differences from other languages: - Dual notation: Both (+ 1 2) and 1 + 2 work for operators; function calls use prefix (println x) - Mandatory testing: Shadow tests required - Explicit types: Return types always specified - Immutable by default: Use mut for mutation

docs.title: 1. Type Along
docs.text: Don't just read - type the examples! Muscle memory helps learning stick.

docs.title: 2. Experiment
docs.text: Modify the examples. Break things. See what error messages you get. This builds intuition.

docs.title: 3. Use Shadow Tests
docs.text:
Write tests as you learn. They'll catch mistakes and reinforce concepts.

fn double(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x 2)
}

shadow double {
    assert (== (double 5) 10)
    assert (== (double 0) 0)
}

docs.title: 4. Read Examples
docs.text: The examples/ directory has production-quality code. Reading it teaches idiomatic patterns.

docs.title: 5. Start Small
docs.text:
Build simple programs first. Gradually add complexity.

Learning progression: 1. Calculator (arithmetic, functions) 2. Todo list (arrays, structs, file I/O) 3. HTTP server (networking, modules) 4. Game (SDL, event handling, state management)

docs.title: Common Questions
docs.text:
Q: Do I need to know C? A: No! Nanolang hides C details. But knowing C helps with FFI and performance tuning.

Q: Can I use nanolang for production? A: Yes! It compiles to native code with excellent performance. Used for games, tools, and applications.

Q: How do I install packages? A: Currently manual. Copy modules to modules/ directory. Package manager coming soon!

Q: Is there an IDE? A: Syntax highlighting available for major editors. LSP server in development for full IDE support.

Q: How fast is nanolang? A: Same as C (it compiles to C). Typical benchmarks show 90-100% of hand-written C performance.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Complete Tutorial 1 - Get comfortable with basics
  2. Try examples - Run programs in examples/
  3. Build something - Start a small project
  4. Read MODULES.md - Explore available libraries
  5. Join the community - Share your creations!

Happy learning! 🚀

docs.title: Tutorial 1: Getting Started with Nanolang
docs.text: Welcome to nanolang! This tutorial will get you up and running in minutes.

docs.title: What is Nanolang?
docs.text: Nanolang is a minimal, LLM-friendly programming language that: - Compiles to native code via C - Has an interpreter for rapid development - Features mandatory testing (shadow tests) - Supports both prefix (+ a b) and infix a + b notation for operators - Supports modern features (generics, pattern matching, FFI)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• GCC or Clang compiler
  • Make build system
  • Git

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/jordanhubbard/nanolang.git
cd nanolang
make

This builds: - bin/nanoc - The compiler (transpiles to C) - bin/nano - The interpreter (direct execution)

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
./bin/nano --version
./bin/nanoc --version

docs.title: Your First Program
docs.text:
Create hello.nano:

fn greet(name: string) -> void {
    (println (string_concat "Hello, " name))
}

fn main() -> int {
    (greet "World")
    return 0
}

shadow greet {
    assert (== (greet "Test") void)
}

docs.title: Running with the Interpreter
docs.text:
./bin/nano hello.nano

Output: 

Hello, World

docs.title: Compiling to Native Code
docs.text:
./bin/nanoc hello.nano -o hello
./hello

Same output, but now it's a native executable!

docs.title: Prefix and Infix Notation
docs.text:
Nanolang supports both prefix notation (like Lisp) and infix notation for operators:

// Prefix notation (original style)
(+ 2 3)          // 5
(* (+ 1 2) 4)    // 12

// Infix notation (also valid!)
2 + 3             // 5
(1 + 2) * 4      // 12

// Comparison (both styles work)
(< 5 10)         // true
5 < 10           // true
(== x y)         // equality check
x == y           // equality check

// Function calls always use prefix
(println "Hello")
(string_concat "Hello" " World")

Note: All infix operators have equal precedence and are evaluated left-to-right. Use parentheses to group: a * (b + c). Unary not and - work without parens: not flag, -x.

docs.title: Type System
docs.text:
Nanolang is statically typed:

let x: int = 42
let y: float = 3.14
let name: string = "Alice"
let flag: bool = true

Type inference works for simple cases:

let z = (+ 1 2)  // inferred as int

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions are defined with fn:

fn add(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (+ a b)
}

fn greet(name: string) -> void {
    (println name)
}

Return types are mandatory - even void for functions with no return value.

docs.title: Shadow Tests (Mandatory Testing)
docs.text:
Every function can have a shadow test block:

fn multiply(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    return (* a b)
}

shadow multiply {
    assert (== (multiply 2 3) 6)
    assert (== (multiply 0 5) 0)
    assert (== (multiply -2 4) (- 8))
}

Shadow tests: - Run automatically during compilation - Ensure code correctness - Are self-documenting - Catch regressions early

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
let x: int = 10           // Immutable by default
let mut y: int = 5        // Mutable variable
let y = (+ y 1)           // Shadowing (creates new binding)
set y (+ y 1)             // Mutation (requires 'mut')

docs.title: Conditionals
docs.text:
fn abs(x: int) -> int {
    if (< x 0) {
        return (- x)
    } else {
        return x
    }
}

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
fn count_to_n(n: int) -> void {
    let mut i: int = 0
    while (< i n) {
        (println (int_to_string i))
        set i (+ i 1)
    }
}

docs.title: Arrays
docs.text:
let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
let first: int = (get numbers 0)
let length: int = (len numbers)

let mut arr: array<int> = []
let arr = (push arr 42)  // Arrays are immutable - returns new array

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you understand the basics:

  1. Tutorial 2: Language Fundamentals - Deeper dive into types, control flow, and data structures
  2. Tutorial 3: Module System - Organizing code and using external libraries
  3. Examples - Browse working examples

docs.title: Built-in Functions
docs.text:
// Console I/O
(println str)              // Print with newline
(print str)                // Print without newline

// String operations
(string_concat a b)        // Concatenate strings
(string_length str)        // Get length
(substring str start len)  // Extract substring

// Type conversions
(int_to_string n)          // Convert int to string
(string_to_int str)        // Convert string to int
(float_to_string f)        // Convert float to string

// Array operations
(len array)                // Get array length
(get array index)          // Get element
(push array value)         // Append element (returns new array)

docs.title: Compilation Options
docs.text:
# Compile to executable
./bin/nanoc program.nano -o program

# Compile with verbose output
./bin/nanoc program.nano -o program -v

# Run with interpreter
./bin/nano program.nano

# Compile and run all tests
make test

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Documentation: docs/FEATURES.md - Complete language reference
  • Examples: examples/ - Working code samples
  • Issues: GitHub Issues for questions and bug reports
  • Modules: docs/MODULES.md - Available libraries

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text:
❌ Wrong: Missing type annotation 

let x = 2 + 3  // ERROR: Missing type annotation

✅ Correct: Both prefix and infix work with type annotations 

let x: int = (+ 2 3)   // prefix notation
let y: int = 2 + 3     // infix notation (also valid!)

❌ Wrong: Missing return type 

fn greet(name: string) {  // ERROR: No return type
    (println name)
}

✅ Correct: Explicit return type 

fn greet(name: string) -> void {
    (println name)
}

❌ Wrong: Mutating immutable variable 

let x: int = 5
set x 10  // ERROR: x is not mutable

✅ Correct: Declare as mutable 

let mut x: int = 5
set x 10  // OK

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
You now know enough to write simple nanolang programs! Continue to Tutorial 2: Language Fundamentals to learn about:

  • Structs and enums
  • Generic types
  • Pattern matching
  • Higher-order functions
  • Error handling with union types

Happy coding! 🚀

docs.title: Tutorial 2: Language Fundamentals
docs.text: This tutorial covers nanolang's core features in depth: structs, enums, generics, and functional programming.

docs.title: Structs (Product Types)
docs.text:
Structs group related data together:

struct Point {
    x: float
    y: float
}

fn distance(p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float {
    let dx: float = (- p2.x p1.x)
    let dy: float = (- p2.y p1.y)
    return (sqrt (+ (* dx dx) (* dy dy)))
}

fn main() -> int {
    let origin: Point = Point { x: 0.0, y: 0.0 }
    let point: Point = Point { x: 3.0, y: 4.0 }
    let dist: float = (distance origin point)
    (println (float_to_string dist))  // 5.0
    return 0
}

shadow distance {
    let p1: Point = Point { x: 0.0, y: 0.0 }
    let p2: Point = Point { x: 3.0, y: 4.0 }
    assert (== (distance p1 p2) 5.0)
}

docs.title: Struct Access
docs.text:
let p: Point = Point { x: 10.0, y: 20.0 }
let x_coord: float = p.x
let y_coord: float = p.y

docs.title: Nested Structs
docs.text:
struct Rectangle {
    top_left: Point
    bottom_right: Point
}

fn area(rect: Rectangle) -> float {
    let width: float = (- rect.bottom_right.x rect.top_left.x)
    let height: float = (- rect.bottom_right.y rect.top_left.y)
    return (* width height)
}

docs.title: Enums (Sum Types)
docs.text:
Enums represent one of several possible values:

enum Color {
    Red
    Green
    Blue
}

fn color_name(c: Color) -> string {
    match c {
        Color.Red => return "red"
        Color.Green => return "green"
        Color.Blue => return "blue"
    }
}

shadow color_name {
    assert (== (color_name Color.Red) "red")
    assert (== (color_name Color.Blue) "blue")
}

docs.title: Enums with Values
docs.text:
enum Shape {
    Circle(radius: float)
    Rectangle(width: float, height: float)
}

fn area_of_shape(s: Shape) -> float {
    match s {
        Shape.Circle(r) => return (* 3.14159 (* r r))
        Shape.Rectangle(w, h) => return (* w h)
    }
}

docs.title: Union Types (Tagged Unions)
docs.text:
Unions represent one of multiple possible types:

union Result<T, E> {
    Ok(value: T)
    Err(error: E)
}

fn divide(a: int, b: int) -> Result<int, string> {
    if (== b 0) {
        return Result.Err "Division by zero"
    } else {
        return Result.Ok (/ a b)
    }
}

fn main() -> int {
    let result: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 2)
    match result {
        Result.Ok(value) => {
            (println (string_concat "Result: " (int_to_string value)))
        }
        Result.Err(error) => {
            (println (string_concat "Error: " error))
        }
    }
    return 0
}

shadow divide {
    let success: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 2)
    match success {
        Result.Ok(v) => assert (== v 5)
        Result.Err(e) => assert false  // Should not error
    }

    let failure: Result<int, string> = (divide 10 0)
    match failure {
        Result.Ok(v) => assert false  // Should error
        Result.Err(e) => assert (== e "Division by zero")
    }
}

docs.title: Optional Values
docs.text:
A common pattern using unions:

union Option<T> {
    Some(value: T)
    None
}

fn find_index(arr: array<int>, target: int) -> Option<int> {
    let mut i: int = 0
    while (< i (len arr)) {
        if (== (get arr i) target) {
            return Option.Some i
        }
        set i (+ i 1)
    }
    return Option.None
}

docs.title: Generic Types
docs.text:
Generics allow code reuse with type safety:

struct Box<T> {
    value: T
}

fn unbox<T>(b: Box<T>) -> T {
    return b.value
}

fn main() -> int {
    let int_box: Box<int> = Box { value: 42 }
    let str_box: Box<string> = Box { value: "hello" }

    (println (int_to_string (unbox int_box)))    // 42
    (println (unbox str_box))                    // hello
    return 0
}

docs.title: Generic Arrays
docs.text:
fn first<T>(arr: array<T>) -> Option<T> {
    if (== (len arr) 0) {
        return Option.None
    } else {
        return Option.Some (get arr 0)
    }
}

shadow first {
    let nums: array<int> = [1, 2, 3]
    let result: Option<int> = (first nums)
    match result {
        Option.Some(v) => assert (== v 1)
        Option.None => assert false
    }

    let empty: array<int> = []
    let result2: Option<int> = (first empty)
    match result2 {
        Option.Some(v) => assert false
        Option.None => assert true
    }
}

docs.title: Higher-Order Functions
docs.text:
Functions are first-class values in nanolang:

fn apply_twice(f: fn(int) -> int, x: int) -> int {
    return (f (f x))
}

fn double(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x 2)
}

fn main() -> int {
    let result: int = (apply_twice double 3)
    (println (int_to_string result))  // 12
    return 0
}

shadow apply_twice {
    fn inc(x: int) -> int { return (+ x 1) }
    assert (== (apply_twice inc 5) 7)
}

docs.title: Map and Reduce
docs.text:
Built-in higher-order functions for arrays:

fn square(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x x)
}

fn add(acc: int, x: int) -> int {
    return (+ acc x)
}

fn main() -> int {
    let numbers: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

    // Map: apply function to each element
    let squares: array<int> = (map numbers square)
    // squares = [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

    // Reduce: accumulate values
    let sum: int = (reduce numbers 0 add)
    // sum = 15

    (println (int_to_string sum))
    return 0
}

docs.title: Pattern Matching
docs.text:
Pattern matching provides exhaustive handling of variants:

union Message {
    Text(content: string)
    Number(value: int)
    Quit
}

fn process(msg: Message) -> void {
    match msg {
        Message.Text(s) => {
            (println (string_concat "Text: " s))
        }
        Message.Number(n) => {
            (println (string_concat "Number: " (int_to_string n)))
        }
        Message.Quit => {
            (println "Quitting")
        }
    }
}

Exhaustiveness: The compiler ensures all cases are handled!

docs.title: If-Else
docs.text:
fn max(a: int, b: int) -> int {
    if (> a b) {
        return a
    } else {
        return b
    }
}

docs.title: While Loops
docs.text:
fn sum_to_n(n: int) -> int {
    let mut sum: int = 0
    let mut i: int = 1
    while (<= i n) {
        let sum = (+ sum i)
        set i (+ i 1)
    }
    return sum
}

docs.title: For-In Loops (Arrays)
docs.text:
fn print_all(arr: array<string>) -> void {
    let mut i: int = 0
    while (< i (len arr)) {
        (println (get arr i))
        set i (+ i 1)
    }
}

docs.title: Dynamic Arrays
docs.text:
Arrays are dynamically sized and immutable by default:

fn build_array() -> array<int> {
    let mut arr: array<int> = []
    let arr = (push arr 1)
    let arr = (push arr 2)
    let arr = (push arr 3)
    return arr
}

shadow build_array {
    let arr: array<int> = (build_array)
    assert (== (len arr) 3)
    assert (== (get arr 0) 1)
    assert (== (get arr 2) 3)
}

docs.title: Array Operations
docs.text:
let arr: array<int> = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

// Access
let first: int = (get arr 0)
let last: int = (get arr (- (len arr) 1))

// Modify (returns new array)
let arr2: array<int> = (push arr 6)
let arr3: array<int> = (set_at arr 0 99)

// Iterate
let mut i: int = 0
while (< i (len arr)) {
    let elem: int = (get arr i)
    (println (int_to_string elem))
    set i (+ i 1)
}

docs.title: Type Aliases
docs.text:
Create meaningful names for complex types:

type UserId = int
type Username = string
type Coordinate = Point

fn get_user_id(name: Username) -> UserId {
    // Implementation
    return 42
}

docs.title: 1. Use Shadow Tests Extensively
docs.text:
fn factorial(n: int) -> int {
    if (<= n 1) {
        return 1
    } else {
        return (* n (factorial (- n 1)))
    }
}

shadow factorial {
    assert (== (factorial 0) 1)
    assert (== (factorial 1) 1)
    assert (== (factorial 5) 120)
    assert (== (factorial 10) 3628800)
}

docs.title: 2. Prefer Immutability
docs.text:
// ❌ Mutable when not needed
let mut count: int = 0
set count (+ count 1)

// ✅ Use shadowing for transformation
let count: int = 0
let count = (+ count 1)

docs.title: 3. Use Union Types for Error Handling
docs.text:
// ❌ Return magic values
fn find(arr: array<int>, target: int) -> int {
    // Returns -1 if not found
    return (- 1)
}

// ✅ Use Option<T>
fn find(arr: array<int>, target: int) -> Option<int> {
    // Explicit success/failure
    return Option.None
}

docs.title: 4. Leverage Type Inference
docs.text:
// Verbose but clear
let x: int = 42
let y: string = "hello"

// Inferred (use for simple cases)
let x = 42  // int inferred
let y = "hello"  // string inferred

docs.title: Builder Pattern
docs.text:
struct Config {
    debug: bool
    port: int
    host: string
}

fn default_config() -> Config {
    return Config {
        debug: false
        port: 8080
        host: "localhost"
    }
}

fn with_debug(c: Config, enabled: bool) -> Config {
    return Config {
        debug: enabled
        port: c.port
        host: c.host
    }
}

docs.title: Iterator Pattern (Map/Filter/Reduce)
docs.text:
fn process_numbers(nums: array<int>) -> int {
    // Filter evens, square them, sum them
    let evens: array<int> = (filter nums is_even)
    let squares: array<int> = (map evens square)
    return (reduce squares 0 add)
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Continue to Tutorial 3: Module System to learn about: - Importing modules - Creating your own modules - Using the standard library - Working with external C libraries

Or explore: - Examples - Real-world code - Modules Reference - Available libraries - FFI Guide - C integration

docs.title: Tutorial 3: Module System
docs.text: Learn how to organize code into modules, use the standard library, and leverage external libraries.

docs.title: What are Modules?
docs.text: Modules are reusable collections of functions, types, and constants. They help organize code and enable code sharing.

docs.title: Module Types
docs.text:
1. Standard Library (std/) - Built-in utilities
  2. External Modules (modules/) - Community/system libraries
  3. Your Own Modules - Project-specific code

docs.title: Basic Import
docs.text:
import "std/io/stdio.nano" as IO

fn main() -> int {
    (IO.println "Hello from a module!")
    return 0
}

docs.title: Multiple Imports
docs.text:
import "std/io/stdio.nano" as IO
import "std/math/extended.nano" as Math
import "modules/sqlite/sqlite.nano" as DB

fn main() -> int {
    let result: float = (Math.sqrt 16.0)
    (IO.println (IO.float_to_string result))
    return 0
}

docs.title: Selective Imports
docs.text:
Import specific functions instead of the entire module:

from "std/math/extended.nano" import sqrt, pow, sin, cos

fn distance(x: float, y: float) -> float {
    return (sqrt (+ (* x x) (* y y)))
}

docs.title: stdio - Console I/O
docs.text:
import "std/io/stdio.nano" as IO

fn demo_stdio() -> void {
    // Printing
    (IO.println "Hello!")
    (IO.print "No newline")

    // Type conversion
    let num_str: string = (IO.int_to_string 42)
    let float_str: string = (IO.float_to_string 3.14)

    // String building
    let msg: string = (IO.string_concat "Answer: " num_str)
    (IO.println msg)
}

docs.title: collections/stringbuilder - Efficient String Building
docs.text:
import "std/collections/stringbuilder.nano" as SB

fn build_message(name: string, age: int) -> string {
    let sb: StringBuilder = (SB.sb_new)
    let sb = (SB.sb_append sb "Name: ")
    let sb = (SB.sb_append sb name)
    let sb = (SB.sb_append sb ", Age: ")
    let sb = (SB.sb_append sb (int_to_string age))
    return (SB.sb_to_string sb)
}

docs.title: math/extended - Math Operations
docs.text:
import "std/math/extended.nano" as Math

fn calculate() -> void {
    let distance: float = (Math.sqrt 25.0)  // 5.0
    let power: float = (Math.pow 2.0 8.0)   // 256.0
    let angle: float = (Math.sin 1.57)      // ≈1.0

    let max_val: int = (Math.max 10 20)     // 20
    let min_val: int = (Math.min 10 20)     // 10
    let absolute: int = (Math.abs (- 5))    // 5
}

docs.title: SQLite Database
docs.text:
import "modules/sqlite/sqlite.nano" as DB

fn create_and_query() -> int {
    // Open database
    let db: opaque = (DB.nl_sqlite3_open "test.db")

    // Create table
    let sql: string = "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)"
    (DB.nl_sqlite3_exec db sql)

    // Insert data
    let insert: string = "INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice')"
    (DB.nl_sqlite3_exec db insert)

    // Query
    let query: string = "SELECT id, name FROM users"
    let stmt: opaque = (DB.nl_sqlite3_prepare_v2 db query)

    while (== (DB.nl_sqlite3_step stmt) DB.SQLITE_ROW) {
        let id: int = (DB.nl_sqlite3_column_int stmt 0)
        let name: string = (DB.nl_sqlite3_column_text stmt 1)
        (println (string_concat (int_to_string id) ": " name))
    }

    (DB.nl_sqlite3_finalize stmt)
    (DB.nl_sqlite3_close db)
    return 0
}

docs.title: Filesystem Operations
docs.text:
import "modules/filesystem/filesystem.nano" as FS

fn file_demo() -> int {
    // Check if file exists
    if (== (FS.nl_fs_file_exists "data.txt") 1) {
        // Read file
        let content: string = (FS.nl_fs_read_file "data.txt")
        (println content)

        // Get file size
        let size: int = (FS.nl_fs_file_size "data.txt")
        (println (string_concat "Size: " (int_to_string size)))
    } else {
        // Write file
        (FS.nl_fs_write_file "data.txt" "Hello, World!")
    }

    // List files in directory
    let files: array<string> = (FS.nl_fs_list_files "." ".nano")
    let mut i: int = 0
    while (< i (len files)) {
        (println (get files i))
        set i (+ i 1)
    }

    return 0
}

docs.title: SDL Graphics
docs.text:
import "modules/sdl/sdl.nano" as SDL

fn graphics_demo() -> int {
    (SDL.SDL_Init SDL.SDL_INIT_VIDEO)

    let window: opaque = (SDL.SDL_CreateWindow "Demo" 100 100 800 600 0)
    let renderer: opaque = (SDL.SDL_CreateRenderer window (- 1) 0)

    // Main loop
    let mut running: int = 1
    while (== running 1) {
        // Event handling
        let event: opaque = (SDL.SDL_Event_new)
        while (== (SDL.SDL_PollEvent event) 1) {
            if (== (SDL.SDL_Event_get_type event) SDL.SDL_QUIT) {
                let running = 0
            }
        }

        // Render
        (SDL.SDL_SetRenderDrawColor renderer 0 0 0 255)
        (SDL.SDL_RenderClear renderer)
        (SDL.SDL_RenderPresent renderer)
    }

    (SDL.SDL_DestroyRenderer renderer)
    (SDL.SDL_DestroyWindow window)
    (SDL.SDL_Quit)
    return 0
}

docs.title: Simple Module
docs.text:
Create mymath.nano:

// mymath.nano - Custom math utilities

pub fn square(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x x)
}

pub fn cube(x: int) -> int {
    return (* x (* x x))
}

fn helper(x: int) -> int {
    // Private function (no 'pub')
    return (+ x 1)
}

shadow square {
    assert (== (square 5) 25)
    assert (== (square 0) 0)
}

shadow cube {
    assert (== (cube 3) 27)
}

Use it:

import "mymath.nano" as Math

fn main() -> int {
    let sq: int = (Math.square 5)    // 25
    let cb: int = (Math.cube 3)      // 27
    // let x = (Math.helper 1)       // ERROR: helper is private
    return 0
}

docs.title: Module with Types
docs.text:
// geometry.nano

pub struct Circle {
    radius: float
}

pub struct Rectangle {
    width: float
    height: float
}

pub fn circle_area(c: Circle) -> float {
    return (* 3.14159 (* c.radius c.radius))
}

pub fn rectangle_area(r: Rectangle) -> float {
    return (* r.width r.height)
}

shadow circle_area {
    let c: Circle = Circle { radius: 5.0 }
    let area: float = (circle_area c)
    assert (> area 78.0)
    assert (< area 79.0)
}

docs.title: Public vs Private
docs.text:
// Only 'pub' items are accessible from outside
pub fn public_function() -> void {
    (println "Everyone can call this")
}

fn private_function() -> void {
    (println "Only this module can call this")
}

pub struct PublicStruct {
    pub field1: int      // Public field
    field2: int          // Private field (default)
}

docs.title: Re-exports
docs.text:
// math_utils.nano
from "std/math/extended.nano" import sqrt, pow
pub use "std/math/extended.nano" as Math  // Re-export module under a public alias

pub fn hypotenuse(a: float, b: float) -> float {
    return (sqrt (+ (* a a) (* b b)))
}

docs.title: Recommended Project Structure
docs.text:
my_project/
├── src/
│   ├── main.nano          # Entry point
│   ├── lib.nano           # Public API
│   ├── models/
│   │   ├── user.nano
│   │   └── post.nano
│   └── utils/
│       ├── string.nano
│       └── math.nano
├── tests/
│   ├── test_user.nano
│   └── test_utils.nano
└── modules/               # External dependencies
    └── (third-party modules)

docs.title: Multi-File Module Example
docs.text:
models/user.nano: 

pub struct User {
    id: int
    username: string
    email: string
}

pub fn create_user(username: string, email: string) -> User {
    return User {
        id: 0
        username: username
        email: email
    }
}

models/post.nano: 

pub struct Post {
    id: int
    title: string
    content: string
    author_id: int
}

pub fn create_post(title: string, content: string, author: int) -> Post {
    return Post {
        id: 0
        title: title
        content: content
        author_id: author
    }
}

main.nano: 

import "models/user.nano" as User
import "models/post.nano" as Post

fn main() -> int {
    let user: User.User = (User.create_user "alice" "alice@example.com")
    let post: Post.Post = (Post.create_post "Hello" "Content" user.id)

    (println user.username)
    (println post.title)
    return 0
}

docs.title: Module Search Paths
docs.text:
Nanolang searches for modules in:

  1. Relative to current file - import "utils/math.nano"
  2. std/ directory - import "std/io/stdio.nano"
  3. modules/ directory - import "modules/sqlite/sqlite.nano"
  4. NANO_MODULE_PATH environment variable

docs.title: 1. One Module Per File
docs.text:
// ✅ Good: Clear, focused module
// user.nano
pub struct User { ... }
pub fn create_user() -> User { ... }

// ❌ Avoid: Kitchen sink module
// everything.nano - contains User, Post, Comment, Tag, etc.

docs.title: 2. Use Descriptive Module Names
docs.text:
// ✅ Clear intent
import "database/connection.nano" as DB
import "utils/string_helpers.nano" as StrUtil

// ❌ Vague names
import "stuff.nano" as X
import "helpers.nano" as H

docs.title: 3. Document Public APIs
docs.text:
// Parse a date string in ISO 8601 format
// Returns Option<Date> - Some(date) if valid, None if invalid
pub fn parse_date(input: string) -> Option<Date> {
    // Implementation
}

docs.title: 4. Keep Modules Cohesive
docs.text:
Each module should have a single responsibility:

  • user.nano - User management
  • auth.nano - Authentication
  • database.nano - Database operations

docs.title: 5. Use Shadow Tests in Modules
docs.text:
pub fn validate_email(email: string) -> bool {
    // Implementation
    return true
}

shadow validate_email {
    assert (== (validate_email "user@example.com") true)
    assert (== (validate_email "invalid") false)
    assert (== (validate_email "") false)
}

docs.title: Service Pattern
docs.text:
// user_service.nano
import "database.nano" as DB

pub struct UserService {
    db: DB.Connection
}

pub fn new_user_service(conn: DB.Connection) -> UserService {
    return UserService { db: conn }
}

pub fn find_user(service: UserService, id: int) -> Option<User> {
    // Implementation
}

docs.title: Factory Pattern
docs.text:
// config.nano
pub struct Config {
    debug: bool
    port: int
}

pub fn production_config() -> Config {
    return Config { debug: false, port: 80 }
}

pub fn development_config() -> Config {
    return Config { debug: true, port: 3000 }
}

docs.title: Import Syntax
docs.text:
// Named import
import "path/to/module.nano" as Name

// Selective import
from "path/to/module.nano" import func1, func2, Type

// Multiple imports
import "module1.nano" as M1
import "module2.nano" as M2

docs.title: Visibility Modifiers
docs.text:
pub fn public_func() -> void { }    // Exported
fn private_func() -> void { }       // Internal only

pub struct PublicStruct { }         // Exported
struct PrivateStruct { }            // Internal only

docs.title: Module Paths
docs.text:
"std/io/stdio.nano"                 // Standard library
"modules/sqlite/sqlite.nano"        // External module
"./local_module.nano"               // Relative path
"../parent_module.nano"             // Parent directory

Happy modular programming! 🎯
